proposals.title: Scaling Down Influence: EK-FAC Analysis for Generalization in Small, On-Device Language Models
proposals.motivation: The deployment of small, efficient language models on edge devices is accelerating, driven by privacy and latency needs. However, our understanding of how these models generalize and which training examples most influence their outputs remains limited compared to large-scale models. Pre-2026, influence estimation methods like EK-FAC have been scaled up to study large models, but their application to the constrained, heterogeneous training regimes of small models is unexplored, creating a gap in responsible, value-aligned deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the EK-FAC influence estimation framework to small, on-device models will reveal distinct patterns of generalization and example influence compared to large models, characterized by stronger reliance on specific data subsets and heightened sensitivity to value-laden training examples.
proposals.method: We will adapt the EK-FAC (Empirical K-Fac) method, a scalable second-order optimization technique for estimating training example influence, to models like TinyLlama or Gemma-2B. The study will involve: 1) fine-tuning these models on curated datasets mixing general tasks and value-sensitive prompts (e.g., from 'Values in the Wild'), 2) computing EK-FAC influence scores for model predictions on held-out evaluation sets, and 3) analyzing correlations between high-influence examples, model performance on specific task categories, and shifts in output distributions, extending Durmus's prior evaluation frameworks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of training data influence for small, on-device LLMs, offering insights into their generalization mechanics and reliability. It will yield practical guidance for data curation and fine-tuning to improve model robustness and value alignment in resource-constrained settings.
proposals.novelty: While EK-FAC has been used for large models, its targeted application to study the generalization and value-relevant behavior of small, efficient models is novel. It uniquely bridges scalable influence estimation with the practical concerns of on-device AI deployment and value alignment.

proposals.title: Evaluating Faithfulness Without Exact Matches: Entailment-Based Metrics for Real-World LLM Outputs
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of LLM outputs in real-world tasks often relies on brittle string matching, which fails to capture semantic equivalence and penalizes valid paraphrases or restatements. This is a critical limitation for analyzing the reliability and values expressed in LLM interactions, as seen in studies of economic tasks and user conversations, where faithful but non-identical responses are common. A more robust, semantics-aware evaluation framework is needed to accurately assess model performance and alignment in the wild.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using natural language inference (NLI) and paraphrase detection models to assess the semantic faithfulness of an LLM's output to a reference or a source context will yield more reliable, human-aligned evaluation scores than rigid string-matching metrics.
proposals.method: We will develop a benchmark by curating and annotating real-world conversational snippets from sources like the Claude conversations dataset, labeling them for semantic faithfulness. We will implement evaluation metrics using pre-trained NLI models (e.g., DeBERTa-based) and paraphrase detection models (e.g., Sentence-BERT) to score outputs. These will be systematically compared against traditional metrics (e.g., ROUGE, exact match) through correlation analyses with human judgments of faithfulness, accuracy, and value adherence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, open-source toolkit for semantics-based faithfulness evaluation, directly applicable to analyzing real-world LLM interactions. It will offer new insights into the reliability of LLM outputs in economic and social tasks, moving beyond surface-level metrics to better capture how models convey information and values.
proposals.novelty: While NLI is used in some QA tasks, its systematic application to evaluate faithfulness in open-ended, real-world conversational outputs—particularly for value-laden or economic content—is novel. This approach directly relaxes the constraint of explicit answer matching, a core limitation in current large-scale interaction analysis.

proposals.title: Faithfulness in the Wild: Extending QA-Based Evaluation to Dialogue and Data-to-Text Generation
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are deployed for complex, open-ended tasks like dialogue and report generation, ensuring their outputs are factually faithful to source information is a critical unsolved problem. Pre-2026 faithfulness metrics like FEQA are designed for summarization, leaving a significant evaluation gap for these interactive and structured-input tasks, which are central to real-world AI adoption as shown in Durmus's work on economic tasks and values in model interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: A QA-based faithfulness framework can be successfully adapted and validated for dialogue and data-to-text generation, revealing systematic patterns of hallucination distinct from those observed in summarization and correlating with human judgments of reliability.
proposals.method: We will adapt the FEQA framework by designing task-specific question generation models. For dialogue, we will generate questions from the conversation history and knowledge sources; for data-to-text (e.g., from tables/JSON), questions will be derived from the structured input. We will use a high-performance QA model (e.g., based on T5 or DeBERTa) to answer these questions from both the source and the generated text, computing an F1-based faithfulness score. The approach will be validated through large-scale automated evaluation on datasets like FaithDial and ToTTo, supplemented with targeted human evaluations to calibrate the metric.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first generalizable, automated faithfulness metrics for dialogue and data-to-text generation, enabling scalable reliability audits. It will contribute a new dataset of (generated text, source, QA pairs) for these tasks and offer empirical analysis of how hallucination patterns manifest differently across generation modalities.
proposals.novelty: While FEQA is established for summarization, its principled extension to interactive dialogue and structured data generation is novel. The research uniquely intersects rigorous automated evaluation with the study of real-world task performance, a core theme in Durmus's prior work on values and economic AI use.

proposals.title: Generating Faithful and Diverse Questions: A Value-Aware, Human-in-the-Loop Framework for Summary Evaluation
proposals.motivation: FEQA (Factual Error Question Answering) is a key benchmark for evaluating the factual consistency of abstractive summaries, but its effectiveness is limited by the quality and coverage of its automatically generated questions. Pre-2026 methods often produce shallow or repetitive questions, failing to probe nuanced factual claims or latent values (e.g., fairness, causality) embedded in summaries. This gap hinders reliable, real-world assessment of LLM-generated content.
proposals.hypothesis: A question-generation framework that explicitly models the latent values and factual dimensions of a source document, guided by human feedback on question utility, will produce more comprehensive and diverse questions, leading to a more reliable and sensitive FEQA-style evaluation metric.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pipeline. First, we will use a fine-tuned LLM (e.g., Claude, as studied in the researcher's prior work) to decompose source documents into a structured set of 'factual dimensions' and associated 'value tags' (e.g., numerical precision, causal attribution, comparative fairness). Second, we will train a question generator (based on a T5 or GPT-2 architecture) conditioned on this structured representation and the summary, using reinforcement learning with a reward function shaped by human evaluators' ratings on question relevance, answerability, and diversity. This human-in-the-loop evaluation protocol mirrors the researcher's established methodology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, value-aware framework for generating diagnostic questions for text evaluation, 2) a new, high-quality dataset of source-summary-question triplets with human annotations for question utility, and 3) an improved FEQA-style metric that demonstrates higher correlation with human judgment of factual consistency on diverse, real-world texts.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior question generation for QA, which focuses on answer span location, our method explicitly models the latent factual and value structure of the source to generate diagnostically useful questions for evaluation, integrating human feedback on utility directly into the training objective.

proposals.title: Faithfulness in the Wild: A Large-Scale, Multi-Domain Dataset for Fine-Grained Evaluation of LLM Factual Consistency
proposals.motivation: Current faithfulness benchmarks for LLMs are often small, domain-restricted, and lack fine-grained error taxonomies, limiting our understanding of how and why models generate unfaithful content in real-world applications. As LLMs are increasingly deployed for complex tasks like summarization, question answering, and content generation, systematic failures in factual consistency pose significant risks to reliability and trust. A more comprehensive dataset is needed to rigorously evaluate and improve model truthfulness across diverse contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a large-scale, multi-domain dataset with fine-grained human annotations of faithfulness errors will reveal systematic patterns in LLM failures, enabling more robust evaluation and providing a critical resource for training and aligning models to be more factually consistent.
proposals.method: We will collect a diverse corpus of LLM outputs (e.g., from Claude, GPT-4) across tasks like summarization of news and scientific articles, long-form Q&A, and instruction-following. Using a taxonomy of faithfulness errors (e.g., intrinsic/extrinsic hallucination, incorrect entity/relation, omission), we will conduct large-scale, high-quality human annotation via a structured platform. We will analyze the resulting dataset to identify failure modes and use it to benchmark state-of-the-art LLMs and automated faithfulness metrics (e.g., question-answering-based, NLI-based).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the research community with a major, publicly available benchmark for faithfulness evaluation, significantly larger and more nuanced than existing datasets. It will yield new insights into the contextual and task-dependent nature of LLM hallucinations, informing the development of more reliable models and evaluation methods.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work focused on narrow domains or coarse labels, this proposal creates a large-scale dataset spanning multiple real-world tasks with a detailed, human-validated error taxonomy, directly connecting to the analysis of 'values in the wild' and real-world model use.

proposals.title: Entailment as a Faithfulness Probe: A Fine-Grained, Model-Agnostic Framework for Evaluating Summary Hallucination
proposals.motivation: Evaluating the factual faithfulness of LLM-generated summaries remains a critical, unsolved challenge. Existing metrics like ROUGE correlate poorly with factual consistency, and human evaluation is costly and non-scalable. A robust, automated method for detecting hallucinations is essential for deploying LLMs in high-stakes domains like medicine, law, and science, where factual accuracy is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that textual entailment, framed as a fine-grained, sentence-to-source verification task, provides a more reliable and interpretable signal for summary faithfulness than n-gram overlap or learned metrics. Furthermore, we hypothesize that the entailment judgments of large, instruction-tuned LLMs (like Claude) can serve as a strong, model-agnostic baseline for this task.
proposals.method: We will construct a dataset by decomposing summaries into atomic factual claims and pairing each with relevant source text spans. We will then apply a two-tier evaluation: 1) Using established Natural Language Inference (NLI) models (e.g., DeBERTa, fine-tuned on MNLI) to generate entailment labels for each claim. 2) Using instruction-tuned LLMs (specifically Claude, via API) in a zero-shot or few-shot prompting setup to perform the same entailment classification, treating it as a structured reasoning task. We will compare these automated scores against expert human annotations of faithfulness on established benchmarks like SummEval and FRANK.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, fine-grained, and interpretable framework for faithfulness evaluation that moves beyond surface-level similarity. It will provide empirical evidence on the reliability of LLMs as entailment-based faithfulness judges, contributing to the literature on LLM self-evaluation and calibration. The resulting methodology and analysis will offer practical guidance for developers needing to audit model outputs in real-world applications.
proposals.novelty: While NLI has been used for fact-checking, its systematic application as a fine-grained, sentence-level probe for summary hallucination is under-explored. The novelty lies in the direct comparison of traditional NLI models against state-of-the-art LLMs (Claude) for this specific task, and in framing the problem to yield interpretable, claim-by-claim error analysis rather than a single composite score.

proposals.title: Semantic Role Labeling for Faithfulness: A Structured Framework for Evaluating Factual Consistency in LLM Summaries
proposals.motivation: Evaluating the factual consistency of summaries generated by large language models (LLMs) remains a critical, unsolved challenge. Existing automated metrics, such as ROUGE or BERTScore, often fail to detect subtle factual contradictions or hallucinations, while human evaluation is costly and non-scalable. A more structured, semantic-level analysis is needed to reliably assess faithfulness, especially as LLMs are deployed in high-stakes domains like news, science, and medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that comparing predicate-argument structures (i.e., 'who did what to whom') extracted via Semantic Role Labeling (SRL) from a source document and its LLM-generated summary provides a more robust and interpretable signal for detecting factual inconsistencies than surface-level lexical or embedding-based similarity metrics.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline that first applies a state-of-the-art SRL model (e.g., AllenNLP's pre-2026 BERT-based model) to both source and summary texts to extract structured semantic frames. We will then design a set of alignment and scoring rules to compare these frames, identifying mismatches in core arguments (Agent, Patient, etc.), predicate negation, or hallucinated events. The system's performance will be benchmarked against existing datasets (e.g., SummEval, FRANK) and validated through human evaluations of inconsistency judgments, following Durmus's established methodology for analyzing real-world LLM interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, interpretable, and automated framework for faithfulness evaluation that operates on explicit semantic representations. It will provide new insights into the specific types of semantic errors LLMs make during summarization. The resulting metric and analysis will advance the field of reliable LLM evaluation, a core theme in Durmus's research on values and reliability in real-world AI use.
proposals.novelty: While SRL has been used in some NLP tasks, its systematic application as a primary framework for evaluating factual consistency in LLM summaries is novel. This approach moves beyond token or sentence-level similarity to a structured comparison of event semantics, offering a more granular and explainable diagnostic tool compared to end-to-end neural metrics.

proposals.title: Stress-Testing Faithfulness: A Controlled Generation Framework for Unfaithful Summaries to Evaluate LLM-Based Metrics
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on LLM summarization heavily relies on automated metrics like BERTScore, FactCC, and LLM-as-a-judge to assess faithfulness. However, the robustness of these metrics against diverse, systematic types of unfaithfulness is poorly understood. This creates a critical gap, as unreliable evaluations undermine trust in deployed summarization systems and their real-world impact on information integrity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current LLM-based faithfulness metrics are brittle and fail to detect subtle, semantically plausible types of unfaithfulness, such as pragmatic implicatures, value-laden framing, and factual distortions on under-represented entities.
proposals.method: We will develop a controlled text generation framework using instruction-tuned LLMs (e.g., Claude or GPT-4) to produce summaries with specific, annotated unfaithfulness categories (e.g., addition, omission, distortion, value shift). This builds on contrastive and counterfactual prompting techniques from pre-2026 work. We will then systematically evaluate a suite of faithfulness metrics (including NLI-based, QA-based, and LLM-as-judge methods) on this novel benchmark, analyzing failure modes and correlations with human judgments.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce (1) a publicly available benchmark of systematically generated unfaithful summaries with fine-grained error taxonomy, and (2) a comprehensive diagnostic analysis of metric failures, providing concrete guidance for improving faithfulness evaluation. It directly extends Durmus's prior work on analyzing values and reliability in real-world LLM interactions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies unfaithfulness in model outputs, our approach proactively engineers a diverse spectrum of unfaithful summaries to stress-test metrics, moving from passive analysis to active, controlled experimentation. This inverse, generative perspective on evaluation is underexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: The Impossibility of a Global Consensus Model: Quantifying Value Conflicts and Representational Trade-offs in LLMs
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in AI development aims to create a single, general-purpose language model for global use. However, pre-2026 work on values in LLMs (e.g., 'Values in the Wild') shows that models encode specific cultural and normative biases. This pursuit risks marginalizing minority viewpoints and imposing a homogenized worldview, raising critical ethical and representational concerns.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a core set of contested socio-political and ethical topics, a single model cannot produce a probability distribution over answers that is simultaneously representative of diverse global populations and internally consistent, due to fundamental, quantifiable conflicts in underlying value structures.
proposals.method: Building on methods from 'Reliability of Distribution Predictions by LLMs', we will first curate a dataset of value-laden prompts where global opinion is known to be heterogeneous, sourced from cross-cultural surveys and real-world interactions (e.g., from Claude conversation data). We will then fine-tune or prompt a base model (like Claude) to simulate distinct 'value personas' representing different demographic or cultural groups. By analyzing the divergence in the output probability distributions and the counterintuitive pseudo-distributions generated when forcing a single 'global' answer, we will formalize the trade-offs using metrics from social choice theory and distributional conflict.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, empirical framework for diagnosing value conflicts in LLM outputs, moving beyond qualitative analysis. It will contribute a methodology for auditing representational equity and offer evidence that the goal of a single global model may be fundamentally at odds with pluralism, arguing for a portfolio or configurable approach to model deployment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies value biases, this proposal novelly frames the problem as an impossibility result for a single model's probability distribution, leveraging technical insights from distributional reliability and large-scale interaction data to quantify representational trade-offs.

proposals.title: Cross-Model and Cross-Lingual Auditing of Value Alignment and Task Performance in LLMs
proposals.motivation: Prior work, such as 'Values in the Wild' and 'Which Economic Tasks are Performed with AI?', has pioneered frameworks for measuring values and real-world task use in specific models like Claude. However, the generalizability of these findings across the rapidly diversifying LLM ecosystem and for non-English languages remains unknown. This is critical for understanding the global impact and potential biases of deployed AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that significant, systematic disparities exist in value expression and task-handling reliability across different model architectures (e.g., decoder-only vs. mixture-of-experts) and languages, which are not captured by evaluations on a single model or in English alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the measurement frameworks from Durmus's prior work to a curated set of models (e.g., GPT-4, Llama 2, PaLM 2, and open-source alternatives) and languages (e.g., Spanish, Mandarin, Arabic, Swahili). For value auditing, we will adapt the 'Values in the Wild' interaction corpus and analysis pipeline. For task and reliability analysis, we will translate and culturally adapt prompts from the 'Economic Tasks' and 'Reliability of Distribution Predictions' studies, employing the same statistical methods (e.g., calibration metrics, entropy analysis) for cross-model comparison.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale, comparative audit of value alignment and functional reliability across major LLM families and languages. It will provide actionable benchmarks for developers and policymakers, highlighting specific areas where model performance or safety degrades for non-dominant languages and architectures.
proposals.novelty: While individual studies audit single models or tasks, this proposal systematically combines frameworks for values, economic utility, and prediction reliability into a unified comparative evaluation across the two most critical axes of variation: model type and human language.

proposals.title: Tracing the Roots of Representational Bias: A Causal Analysis of Training Data and Architectural Factors in LLM Opinion Distributions
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly mediate global information access and discourse, yet their outputs often reflect skewed representations of worldviews, disproportionately favoring opinions from Western, English-speaking sources. This representational bias, documented in studies of real-world LLM use, can perpetuate cultural marginalization and undermine the reliability of AI for global tasks. A systematic, causal understanding of the factors—from data curation to model design—that produce these biases is urgently needed to develop more equitable systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that representational biases in LLM outputs are not solely a function of raw data demographics but are causally amplified by specific architectural choices (e.g., attention mechanisms, layer norms) and training procedures (e.g., objective functions, data mixing ratios) that selectively amplify signals from dominant cultural groups in the pre-training corpus.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled ablation study using the Pythia model suite and similar open-source architectures to isolate variables. First, we will synthetically construct training datasets with known, quantifiable opinion distributions across simulated cultural dimensions. We will then train or fine-tune models, varying key factors: data mixing ratios, the choice of normalization layer (LayerNorm vs. RMSNorm), and the attention masking strategy. We will employ causal mediation analysis and probing techniques (e.g., linear probes on intermediate representations) to trace how specific model components transform input distributions. Evaluation will use the framework from 'Reliability of Distribution Predictions' to measure distributional skews in outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causal map linking specific technical decisions in model development to measurable representational biases in outputs. It will offer concrete, evidence-based guidelines for mitigating bias through data and architectural interventions. The findings will directly inform the design of more globally representative LLMs and advance methodologies for auditing model values, building on Durmus's prior work in 'Values in the Wild'.
proposals.novelty: While prior work documents bias, our approach uniquely employs controlled synthetic data and causal mediation analysis on model internals to move from correlation to causation, isolating the mechanistic drivers of skewed representation beyond simple data counts.

proposals.title: Dual Alignment: Measuring the Dynamic Convergence of Human and LLM Opinions in Real-World Tasks
proposals.motivation: As LLMs like Claude become integrated into daily tasks, they act not just as tools but as persuasive communicators of opinions and values. Prior work by Durmus et al. (2025) on 'Values in the Wild' and economic task usage shows models are deeply embedded in human decision-making, yet we lack longitudinal studies on how this exposure influences human belief systems. Understanding this dynamic is critical for assessing the societal impact of AI, from polarization risks to the erosion of independent human judgment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that repeated exposure to an LLM's opinions on subjective, value-laden tasks will lead to a measurable increase in the similarity between a human user's subsequent opinions and the model's stated positions, with the effect size modulated by the user's initial agreement and the perceived model authority on the task.
proposals.method: We will design a longitudinal, within-subjects experiment using a platform similar to the one in Durmus's 'Economic Tasks' study. Participants will complete multi-session opinion-elicitation tasks (e.g., policy evaluations, ethical dilemmas) with an intervention group receiving explanations from a fixed model (Claude 3 Opus). We will quantify opinion similarity using embedding-based semantic similarity (Sentence-BERT) and distributional metrics inspired by Durmus's work on 'Reliability of Distribution Predictions'. Statistical analysis will employ mixed-effects models to track similarity drift over time and sessions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first longitudinal, quantitative evidence of LLM-driven opinion alignment in ecologically valid settings. It will contribute a novel methodological framework for measuring human-model belief convergence and offer empirical insights into the conditions under which AI acts as a persuasive agent, directly informing the design of human-AI interaction and AI governance.
proposals.novelty: While existing work analyzes static values or task usage, this proposal dynamically measures the *shift* in human opinions toward a model's stance over time, combining Durmus's expertise in real-world interaction analysis with a novel longitudinal, similarity-tracking design.

proposals.title: Targeted Immunization: Curating Core Antigenic Datasets for Robust, Domain-Specialized Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Current foundation models are trained on vast, general corpora, which may dilute domain-specific reasoning patterns and lead to brittle performance on specialized tasks. This research challenges the prevailing assumption that scale and breadth are always optimal, especially for high-stakes domains like law and medicine where accuracy and fidelity are paramount. It investigates whether a more focused, biologically-inspired training paradigm can yield superior expertise.
proposals.hypothesis: Intensive pre-training on a small, meticulously curated 'core antigenic dataset' that encapsulates the fundamental syntactic, semantic, and logical structures of a specialty, followed by adversarial 'booster' training, will produce a model with more robust, accurate, and deeply internalized domain expertise than a model trained on a larger but more general corpus.
proposals.method: We will construct a core antigenic dataset for legal reasoning, curating texts that exemplify formal logic, statutory interpretation, and case-based reasoning. Using a pre-trained base model (e.g., LLaMA-2 or Claude), we will perform continued pre-training exclusively on this core set. We will then implement a 'booster' phase using adversarial filtering to identify and train on rare, challenging edge cases from the same domain. Performance will be evaluated against a control model trained on a larger, general legal corpus using established benchmarks (e.g., LegalBench) and novel evaluations of reasoning chain fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for or against the 'broad data is best' paradigm in specialization, introducing a novel, immunology-inspired framework for model training. It will contribute a methodology for constructing antigenic datasets and a rigorous evaluation of reasoning robustness, directly informing more efficient and reliable AI deployment in specialized fields.
proposals.novelty: While continued pre-training on domain data is common, the novelty lies in the immunization analogy—specifically, the hypothesis that a small, structurally fundamental 'antigen' dataset is more effective than a large one, and the strategic use of an adversarial 'booster' phase to consolidate learning, which is distinct from standard fine-tuning or retrieval-augmented generation.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Language Dividend: An Econometric Analysis of Foundation Model Multilinguality on Economic Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the rapid scaling of foundation models has been concentrated in high-resource languages, raising concerns about a widening digital divide. While research like Durmus's work on AI task usage provides evidence of economic impact, there is a critical lack of causal, quantitative evidence on how multilingual capability itself creates value. This gap hinders efficient resource allocation for model development and the formulation of equitable AI policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the introduction or significant improvement of a foundation model's capability in a specific language causes measurable, positive changes in user-level economic outcomes, such as productivity and information access, with effects varying systematically by language resource level and domain.
proposals.method: We will construct a longitudinal, multilingual dataset by expanding a structured resource like WikiLingua to a spectrum of 50+ languages, capturing parallel document creation and editing events over time. Using a difference-in-differences design, we will treat the release of a major model update (e.g., Claude adding a new language) as a quasi-experiment. We will instrument for actual model usage with regional API access data to estimate causal effects on outcome metrics derived from the dataset, such as content volume, complexity, and cross-lingual alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first causal, econometric framework for valuing the 'language dividend' of AI, moving beyond descriptive correlations. It will deliver a novel, open multilingual testbed for evaluating model impact and provide concrete evidence to guide corporate R&D prioritization and public investment in equitable digital infrastructure.
proposals.novelty: This fuses econometric causal inference methods with large-scale multilingual NLP evaluation in a way not previously done, applying the lens of economic value to the technical problem of multilingual model capability.

proposals.title: Verifying Values in the Wild: A Formal Framework for Provable Safety in Few-Shot Adaptation
proposals.motivation: As foundation models are increasingly deployed in diverse, real-world tasks via in-context learning and parameter-efficient fine-tuning, ensuring their safety alignment becomes critical yet resource-intensive. Current safety methods rely on extensive post-hoc auditing and large-scale adversarial training, which are infeasible for low-resource scenarios and new language pairs. A formal, data-efficient approach is needed to provide mathematical guarantees that safety properties persist during rapid adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by analyzing a model's internal mechanisms—specifically, the causal pathways activated during few-shot learning—we can formally verify that core safety properties (e.g., non-toxicity, refusal of harmful instructions) are preserved across adaptations, even with minimal aligned examples.
proposals.method: We will develop a verification framework that combines abstract interpretation of transformer activations with symbolic reasoning over few-shot prompts. First, we will adapt formal methods from neural network verification (e.g., using convex relaxations) to bound the output distribution of a model given a few-shot safety demonstration. Second, we will design a property specification language for safety constraints (e.g., "never output instructions for violence") grounded in pre-2026 taxonomies. Third, we will evaluate the framework on models like Claude, using controlled few-shot adaptations on tasks from the researcher's prior work, measuring verification success against human evaluations of safety breaches.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, formally grounded methodology for safe few-shot learning, reducing the data and computational overhead of alignment. It will produce a publicly available benchmark for verifying safety properties during adaptation and offer new insights into the reliability and internal mechanisms of foundation models when performing new economic or linguistic tasks with limited data.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond post-hoc auditing by integrating formal verification directly into the few-shot learning process, a synergy not yet explored in pre-2026 literature. It uniquely focuses on proving safety for parameter-efficient updates, contrasting with prior work on verifying static models or using large-scale fine-tuning.

proposals.title: The Evaluation Game: A Dynamic, Adversarial Framework for Assessing Language Model Values and Reliability
proposals.motivation: Static benchmark evaluations for language models are increasingly brittle, as models can be optimized to exploit metric-specific weaknesses without genuine improvement, a phenomenon linked to Goodhart's law. This is particularly critical for assessing nuanced properties like value alignment and distributional reliability, where models must perform robustly in open-ended, real-world interactions. A new paradigm is needed to create more resilient and meaningful evaluations that reflect the dynamic, strategic nature of real-world AI deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: Framing NLG evaluation as a dynamic, multi-round game between a metric designer (seeking to detect flaws) and a model generator (seeking to satisfy the metric) will produce more robust and generalizable assessments of model values and reliability than static benchmarks. This adversarial process will surface failure modes that static tests miss.
proposals.method: We will design a game-theoretic framework where the 'metric designer' is an LLM (e.g., Claude) prompted or fine-tuned to generate novel evaluation probes or adversarial examples targeting a specific value (e.g., fairness) or reliability criterion. The 'model generator' is the system under test, which must respond to these probes. Over multiple rounds, the metric designer adapts its probes based on previous generator outputs, simulating an arms race. We will analyze the resulting interaction trajectories, using methods from Durmus's prior work on value discovery and pseudo-distributions to quantify how evaluation robustness and task performance evolve.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, operational framework for dynamic evaluation that moves beyond static benchmarks. It will yield new insights into the specific failure modes and strategic behaviors of LLMs when under adaptive pressure, contributing to more honest assessments of value alignment and predictive reliability. The resulting protocol and datasets will be a tool for future, more rigorous model auditing.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial attacks exist, framing the entire evaluation lifecycle as a co-evolutionary game between metric and model is novel. This approach directly integrates strategic adaptation into the core evaluation philosophy, contrasting with the one-off nature of existing benchmarks and red-teaming exercises.

proposals.title: CrowdControl: Adapting Crowdsourcing Quality Control for Consistent Human Evaluation of Language Model Outputs
proposals.motivation: Human evaluation is a cornerstone of NLP research, especially for assessing nuanced qualities like safety, helpfulness, and alignment with human values. However, as shown in pre-2026 work like 'Values in the Wild', these judgments are often noisy and inconsistent, threatening the reliability of conclusions about model capabilities and societal impact. This inconsistency limits our ability to rigorously compare models and understand their real-world effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that established statistical quality control methods from the crowdsourcing literature—such as expectation maximization for inferring true labels from noisy annotators (e.g., Dawid-Skene) and attention-check mechanisms—can be systematically adapted to significantly improve the consistency and reliability of human judgments in NLG evaluation tasks.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled study where human evaluators assess outputs from models like Claude and GPT-4 on tasks from Durmus's prior work (e.g., value-laden dialogues, economic reasoning). We will implement a pipeline that treats each evaluator as a 'noisy classifier' and applies the Dawid-Skene algorithm to infer a consensus 'gold' label and per-annotator reliability scores. We will compare this against raw majority voting. Furthermore, we will integrate targeted 'honeypot' questions with known answers to filter inattentive workers and calibrate our models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, open-source framework for applying crowdsourcing QC to NLG evaluation, yielding more statistically robust human labels. It will contribute new empirical findings on the specific patterns of annotator disagreement in complex LLM evaluation tasks, moving beyond simple accuracy to model evaluator biases.
proposals.novelty: While crowdsourcing QC is mature, its systematic application to the specific and subjective domain of modern LLM output evaluation—where 'ground truth' is often ill-defined—is novel. This bridges a well-established methodological area with the pressing, unsolved problem of evaluation reliability in contemporary AI research.

proposals.title: Inverse Evaluation: Learning Task-Specific Metrics from Distributions of High-Quality LLM Outputs
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of LLMs relies on human-authored metrics or benchmarks, which are costly, often narrow, and may not capture the full spectrum of desirable qualities in model outputs. This creates a bottleneck for assessing performance on novel or complex tasks, especially those involving nuanced values or subjective criteria. An automated method to infer evaluation standards from data would accelerate and broaden the assessment of AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By analyzing the statistical and semantic distributions of outputs deemed high-quality (e.g., from curated datasets or top-performing models), we can automatically learn a set of latent evaluation criteria that correlate with human judgments and generalize to new outputs.
proposals.method: We will leverage large-scale, high-quality conversation data (e.g., from sources like Anthropic's Claude logs) as a corpus of positive examples. Using contrastive learning and distributional analysis techniques (inspired by pre-2026 work on model probing and pseudo-distributions), we will train a metric model to distinguish these outputs from perturbed or lower-quality variants. The approach will involve clustering output features to discover latent evaluation dimensions and validating them against human annotations from prior studies on values and economic tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a framework for data-driven, task-adaptive evaluation metric generation, reducing dependency on pre-defined rubrics. It will contribute new insights into the implicit standards embedded in high-quality LLM behavior and offer scalable tools for auditing model performance on emerging use cases.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes model outputs to understand values or reliability, the inverse problem of systematically deriving the evaluation function itself from output distributions is largely unexplored. This flips the standard paradigm of applying a known metric to instead discover the metric that best explains observed quality.

proposals.title: Cross-Lingual and Cross-Model Analysis of Persona-Driven Value Expression in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Prior work, such as 'Values in the Wild', has established that LLMs express different values when assigned specific personas, but this analysis has been limited primarily to English and a narrow set of models. As LLMs are deployed globally, it is critical to understand whether these value shifts and biases are consistent across languages and model architectures, which has significant implications for fairness, safety, and cultural alignment in real-world applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the expression of values through marked personas will vary significantly across both different language model families (e.g., GPT-4, Claude 3, Llama 2, PaLM 2) and across languages (e.g., Spanish, Mandarin, Arabic), revealing systematic, non-uniform biases that are not apparent in English-only, single-model evaluations.
proposals.method: We will extend the 'Marked Personas' method by applying a consistent set of value-laden prompts (e.g., on fairness, authority, individualism) to a curated panel of LLMs (including proprietary and open-source models available pre-2026) across multiple high-resource languages. The analysis will use automated semantic similarity scoring (e.g., with multilingual sentence embeddings) and human evaluations by native speakers to quantify divergence in value expression from a baseline 'unmarked' assistant persona. Statistical tests will identify significant interactions between model type, language, and persona.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, cross-lingual and cross-model audit of persona-induced value expression in LLMs, mapping the landscape of potential cultural and architectural biases. It will deliver actionable insights for developers and policymakers on which model-language combinations exhibit the most stable or unstable value profiles, informing safer multilingual deployment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes personas or multilingual performance in isolation, this proposal uniquely combines these axes to investigate how the interaction of model architecture and language mediates value expression, moving beyond single-model, English-centric evaluations.

proposals.title: Adversarial Value Alignment: Transferring NLP Fairness Techniques to Mitigate Social Biases in Text-to-Image Generation
proposals.motivation: Recent text-to-image models (e.g., DALL-E 2, Stable Diffusion, Midjourney) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities but also propagate and amplify harmful social biases related to gender, race, and profession. Existing mitigation strategies, such as prompt engineering or post-hoc filtering, are often brittle and fail to address the root cause embedded in the model's parameters. This work addresses the urgent need for robust, train-time debiasing methods for generative vision models, a critical step for their safe and equitable deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that gradient-based adversarial debiasing techniques, proven effective for reducing stereotypical associations in language model representations, can be successfully adapted to disentangle and suppress social biases in the latent space of diffusion-based text-to-image generators, leading to more value-aligned image synthesis without catastrophic forgetting of core generative capabilities.
proposals.method: We will adapt the adversarial training framework from NLP (e.g., the Gradient Reversal Layer approach) to a pre-trained latent diffusion model (LDM). A bias prediction adversary (a shallow classifier) will be attached to intermediate layers of the LDM's text encoder or cross-attention maps, trained to predict protected attributes (e.g., gender) from neutral prompts (e.g., 'a person cooking'). The core generator will be trained to maximize the adversary's loss via gradient reversal, encouraging it to produce representations that are invariant to the bias attribute. We will use human evaluations and established fairness metrics (e.g., bias amplification scores) on generated images to measure success.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide one of the first rigorous applications of NLP-style adversarial fairness techniques to large-scale vision generators, establishing a new technical benchmark for intrinsic debiasing. It will contribute a novel evaluation framework for measuring value alignment in multimodal generative systems, extending Esin Durmus's prior work on analyzing values in LLM interactions to the visual domain.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial debiasing is established in NLP and vision classifiers, its application to *generative* vision models is largely unexplored. The key novelty lies in adapting these techniques to the complex, multi-stage architecture of diffusion models and evaluating their efficacy on open-ended generation tasks, rather than closed-world classification.

proposals.title: Beyond Single-Point Evaluation: A Multi-Style Framework for Assessing Summarization Diversity and Value Alignment
proposals.motivation: Current summarization evaluation relies on a narrow 'gold standard' reference, penalizing valid stylistic or perspectival variations. This misaligns with real-world use, where users have diverse preferences (e.g., concise vs. detailed, conservative vs. liberal framing), as evidenced by studies of values in real-world LLM interactions. A framework that accommodates multiple valid summaries is crucial for accurate model assessment and alignment with heterogeneous human values.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling summary variation across stylistic and value dimensions, we can develop evaluation metrics that are more robust, human-aligned, and informative for model comparison than single-reference metrics like ROUGE.
proposals.method: We will first curate a dataset of multi-style summaries using a human-in-the-loop process: annotators will generate summaries for a document set, guided by diverse style/value prompts (e.g., 'summarize for a policy brief' vs. 'for a technical deep dive'). We will then train a lightweight 'style-value probe' (e.g., a linear classifier on top of a frozen LLM like Claude's embeddings) to predict the style/value profile of any summary. Finally, we will develop a multi-dimensional evaluation metric that scores a candidate summary based on its adherence to a *target* style/value profile, rather than a single reference.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel evaluation framework and benchmark for assessing summarization diversity, 2) a diagnostic tool to analyze which stylistic/value dimensions current LLMs can or cannot reliably produce, and 3) a more nuanced understanding of 'reliability' in LLM generation, moving beyond distributional correctness to controlled stylistic variation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied values in LLM outputs or the reliability of predictions, no framework exists to systematically evaluate LLMs on their ability to generate and adhere to a *spectrum* of pre-defined summary styles tied to human values, relaxing the assumption of a single best output.

proposals.title: Active Value Alignment: Efficient Human Evaluation of LLM-Generated Summaries via Uncertainty and Disagreement Sampling
proposals.motivation: Human evaluation is the gold standard for assessing nuanced LLM outputs like summaries, but it is prohibitively expensive and slow, limiting the scale and frequency of reliable assessments. This bottleneck hinders research into the real-world values and reliability of LLMs, as seen in studies of in-the-wild interactions and economic tasks. An efficient method to select the most informative samples for human judgment is urgently needed to make rigorous, value-sensitive evaluation scalable.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing human evaluation as an active learning problem, where an LLM's own uncertainty estimates and inter-annotator disagreement signals are used to select samples, we can achieve evaluation accuracy comparable to random sampling with a significantly smaller number of human judgments.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage active learning pipeline for summary evaluation. First, we generate a large, diverse corpus of summaries from models like Claude and GPT-4. Second, we implement acquisition functions based on (1) the predictive entropy of a reward model fine-tuned for summary quality, and (2) the variance in scores from a panel of lightweight, specialized critique models (simulating human disagreement). Batches of summaries with the highest acquisition scores will be sent for human evaluation on platforms like Mechanical Turk, with the resulting labels used to iteratively retrain the reward model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, open-source framework for cost-effective human evaluation of LLM text generation. It will yield empirical results on the sample efficiency gains for different evaluation dimensions (e.g., factuality, coherence, alignment with user values) and offer insights into which uncertainty proxies best correlate with human disagreement in value-laden tasks.
proposals.novelty: While active learning is well-established, its application to optimizing human evaluation for LLM alignment is novel. This approach uniquely combines uncertainty sampling from a reward model with simulated disagreement sampling, directly addressing the challenge of evaluating subjective, value-sensitive outputs highlighted in pre-2026 alignment research.

proposals.title: Summaries as a Diagnostic Lens: Evaluating Pretraining Data Composition and Quality via Model-Generated Summaries
proposals.motivation: Understanding the composition and quality of massive, often opaque, pretraining datasets is a critical challenge for interpreting and improving large language models (LLMs). Current methods for dataset analysis are either static (e.g., manual inspection of samples) or indirect (e.g., probing tasks). A more dynamic, task-oriented diagnostic tool is needed to reveal how data characteristics functionally impact model capabilities, particularly for high-level tasks like summarization that require synthesis and coherence.
proposals.hypothesis: The quality and characteristics of an LLM's generated summaries for documents from specific domains (e.g., scientific, legal, narrative) are a sensitive diagnostic of the quality, quantity, and representativeness of that domain's data within its pretraining corpus.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled evaluation framework. First, we will curate a benchmark of source documents spanning diverse domains and quality levels. We will then prompt a target LLM (e.g., Claude variants) to generate summaries for these documents. The core analysis will correlate summary quality metrics (e.g., factual consistency, coherence, relevance via human and automated evaluations like QAFactEval) with known, manipulated properties of the source documents and with external proxies for the model's pretraining data exposure in that domain (e.g., frequency in Common Crawl, presence in known datasets). We will also conduct ablation studies by fine-tuning the model on controlled, domain-specific data mixtures and observing the resultant shifts in summary quality diagnostics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, task-based methodology for auditing pretraining data, moving beyond static analysis. It will yield actionable insights into data-quality bottlenecks for specific capabilities and offer a more interpretable link between dataset composition and downstream model performance. The findings could inform more targeted and efficient data curation strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has evaluated summarization models, using summarization performance as an *inverse diagnostic* to audit the pretraining data itself is a novel, dual-purpose approach. It connects the model's generative behavior on a complex task directly to its foundational data, a link not systematically explored in pre-2026 data auditing research.

proposals.title: Benchmarking the Moving Target: Evaluating the Stability of LLM Values and Capabilities Under Simulated User Interaction
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, LLMs are increasingly deployed in interactive settings where they learn from user feedback, yet their primary evaluation relies on static benchmarks. This creates a critical gap: a model's initial performance on a fixed test may not reflect its evolving behavior, particularly concerning its adherence to stated values or its performance on economic tasks after real-world use. This mismatch poses risks for reliable deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an LLM's performance on static benchmarks for value alignment and task competency is a poor predictor of its behavior after simulated, value-relevant user interactions, and that this divergence can be systematically measured and characterized.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled simulation environment where a model (e.g., Claude) engages in multi-turn dialogues with a population of simulated users, modeled using LLM personas with diverse stated values. We will adapt methods from 'Values in the Wild' to inject value-laden prompts and track behavioral drift. Pre- and post-interaction, we will administer a battery of static benchmarks (e.g., HELM, TruthfulQA, BIG-Bench) and novel 'dynamic consistency' probes. Analysis will involve comparing performance shifts and using techniques from 'Reliability of Distribution Predictions' to assess uncertainty in the model's post-interaction outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical framework for quantifying the 'interaction-induced drift' of LLMs relative to static benchmarks. It will yield concrete metrics for the stability of model values and capabilities, informing more realistic evaluation paradigms and deployment safety checks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes values in real logs or model predictions in isolation, this proposal actively engineers interactive loops to *induce* and measure divergence from static evaluations, directly testing the core assumption of benchmark validity for adaptive systems.

proposals.title: Beyond the Crowd: Developing Theory-Grounded Automated Metrics for Complex LLM Values
proposals.motivation: Human evaluation is the gold standard for assessing complex qualities like values, safety, and social norms in LLM outputs, but it is costly, slow, and suffers from low inter-annotator agreement and cultural biases. This bottleneck impedes scalable, reproducible research and real-time system monitoring. Pre-2026, the field lacks robust, automated alternatives grounded in established social and ethical theory, creating a critical gap in reliable evaluation infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by grounding automated metrics in formal theories from moral philosophy and social science (e.g., contractualism, value pluralism), we can develop proxies that correlate strongly with, and in some dimensions surpass, the reliability and construct validity of aggregated human judgments for complex value-laden tasks.
proposals.method: We will first curate a benchmark of value-laden prompts and LLM responses from public datasets (e.g., Chatbot Arena) and the researcher's prior work on real-world interactions. For each item, we will collect detailed human rationale annotations explaining *why* a judgment was made, linking to theoretical constructs. We will then train a suite of automated evaluators (e.g., fine-tuned LLMs like Claude, or structured prompting with chain-of-thought) to predict both the human rating and, crucially, the theoretical rationale. Performance will be assessed via correlation with held-out human labels and, innovatively, via diagnostic tests of the evaluators' consistency with the underlying theoretical principles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a framework and open-source tools for developing theory-grounded automated evaluation, moving beyond black-box scoring. It will yield new insights into which value dimensions are more amenable to automation and where human judgment remains irreplaceable, directly informing the design of more reliable and transparent LLM monitoring systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs to simulate human ratings, our approach uniquely emphasizes explicability by tethering metrics to formal theoretical constructs and validating them against human rationales, not just scores. This moves the goal from mimicking crowd-sourced labels to building interpretable, principled measurement instruments.

proposals.title: HELM-Wild: Expanding Holistic Evaluation with Real-World Scenarios and Value-Aligned Metrics
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, LLM evaluation is fragmented between static benchmarks like HELM and dynamic, real-world interaction studies. HELM provides a standardized, multi-metric framework but lacks scenarios derived from actual user behavior and explicit metrics for value alignment in context. This gap limits our understanding of how models perform on tasks people actually use them for and whether their outputs reflect human values in practice.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating real-world interaction data and value-sensitive evaluation dimensions into the HELM framework will reveal significant performance gaps and value misalignments in state-of-the-art LLMs that are not captured by current static benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will first curate new HELM scenarios from a large-scale dataset of anonymized, real-world user-AI conversations (e.g., from sources like the 'Which Economic Tasks...' paper). For each scenario, we will define task-specific success metrics. Second, drawing from methods in 'Values in the Wild', we will design and implement a suite of context-dependent value metrics (e.g., fairness, honesty, helpfulness) applicable across scenarios. Third, we will evaluate a diverse set of LLMs (including Claude, GPT-4, and open-source models) on this expanded HELM-Wild benchmark, analyzing performance and value alignment correlations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a major public update to the HELM benchmark, introducing standardized, reproducible evaluations for real-world tasks and value dimensions. It will provide the community with a more holistic and practically relevant evaluation suite and offer new empirical insights into the relationship between model capabilities, deployment patterns, and value alignment.
proposals.novelty: While HELM is comprehensive and studies like 'Values in the Wild' analyze real interactions, no pre-2026 work systematically bridges the two by formally embedding real-world, value-sensitive tasks into a rigorous, multi-model benchmarking framework. This creates a novel synthesis of large-scale behavioral analysis and holistic evaluation.

proposals.title: Scaling Values: A Systematic Benchmark of Value Alignment and Task Performance Across Model Architectures and Sizes
proposals.motivation: Prior work by Durmus et al. (2024) has analyzed values and economic tasks in large, frontier models like Claude, but the landscape of deployed AI is diversifying. Organizations are increasingly using smaller, more efficient models (e.g., LLaMA-2, Mistral, Phi) and specialized architectures (e.g., mixture-of-experts). A systematic understanding of how value alignment and task performance scale and vary across this broader ecosystem is critically lacking, hindering informed model selection and risk assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that (1) value alignment does not scale monotonically with model size or compute, and (2) specific architectural choices (e.g., decoder-only vs. encoder-decoder) induce distinct, predictable biases in both value expression and task performance on real-world economic activities.
proposals.method: We will extend the methodologies from 'Values in the Wild' and 'Which Economic Tasks are Performed with AI?' to a curated set of ~15 open and closed-source models spanning scales (from 7B to >70B parameters) and architectures (decoder-only, encoder-decoder, mixture-of-experts). Using the established human-in-the-loop framework, we will generate and analyze real-world interaction scenarios. We will employ statistical causal discovery methods (e.g., PC algorithm) on the resulting dataset to disentangle the effects of scale, architecture, and training data on value-relevant outputs and task success rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive benchmark of value alignment across a diverse model ecosystem, providing a crucial map for practitioners and policymakers. It will contribute a new dataset of multi-model interactions and identify architectural features that promote or hinder specific values, informing both safer deployment and future model design.
proposals.novelty: While existing benchmarks focus on capability or abstract ethics, this integrates value discovery from real-world interactions with architectural analysis across a wide model spectrum, moving beyond the exclusive study of frontier LLMs to understand the broader, heterogeneous AI landscape.

proposals.title: Tracing Value Trajectories: A Longitudinal Analysis of Opinion Drift Across Language Model Training Stages
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly deployed in sociotechnical systems, understanding the values and opinions they reflect is critical for alignment and safety. Prior work, like 'Values in the Wild', analyzes static snapshots of deployed models, but we lack a longitudinal understanding of how these stabilities or instabilities emerge. Mapping how opinions form and shift during training is essential for diagnosing the origins of bias, predicting downstream behavior, and enabling more controlled model development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that LM opinions on sociopolitical and ethical issues do not converge monotonically but exhibit significant, non-linear drift during both pre-training and fine-tuning, with fine-tuning inducing more targeted but potentially less predictable shifts than continued pre-training.
proposals.method: We will analyze a series of model checkpoints from a public LM training run (e.g., Pythia, OPT). Using a structured value probe dataset derived from prior surveys (e.g., Political Compass, Moral Foundations Questionnaire), we will elicit model opinions via multiple-choice QA and free-generation prompts, applying the evaluation frameworks from Durmus's prior work. We will quantify drift using distributional similarity metrics (e.g., Jensen-Shannon divergence) between checkpoint outputs and track trajectories for specific value dimensions. For fine-tuning, we will repeat the analysis on instruction-tuned variants (e.g., Alpaca) and RLHF checkpoints (e.g., from Anthropic's Claude research) to isolate stage-specific effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical map of opinion dynamics throughout the LM lifecycle, linking training interventions to specific value shifts. It will contribute a methodological framework for longitudinal model auditing and offer concrete evidence to inform debates on whether values are primarily learned during pre-training or instilled via fine-tuning.
proposals.novelty: While existing work evaluates models at a single point in time, this study introduces a temporal dimension, treating opinion formation as a dynamic process. It uniquely combines the longitudinal analysis of training checkpoints with a fine-grained, value-oriented evaluation schema rooted in the researcher's prior work on real-world interactions.

proposals.title: Dual-Objective Alignment: Optimizing Language Models for Demographic Opinion Representativeness
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to simulate human opinions and values in social science and policy tasks, yet they often reflect the biases and narrow perspectives of their training data. As shown by pre-2026 work on values in real-world interactions, this can lead to outputs that are unrepresentative of diverse populations, undermining their utility and fairness in applications like deliberative polling or economic task assistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that augmenting the standard language modeling objective with a demographic representativeness loss, derived from curated survey data, will produce models whose generated opinions on value-laden topics better match the statistical distribution of opinions across key demographic groups, without sacrificing core linguistic capabilities.
proposals.method: We will formulate a dual-objective loss function: L_total = L_lm + λ * L_rep. L_lm is the standard next-token prediction loss. L_rep will be a distribution-matching loss (e.g., KL divergence) between the model's predicted distribution over stance options for a set of value-laden prompts (e.g., from the World Values Survey) and target distributions derived from high-quality demographic survey datasets (e.g., Pew, YouGov). We will fine-tune a medium-scale model (e.g., Llama-2-7B or Claude Instant) using this combined objective, with λ tuned via a validation set. Representativeness will be evaluated on held-out survey items and real-world conversation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete method for steering LLMs toward demographic representativeness, a measurable dimension of value alignment. It will contribute a new evaluation framework for assessing the distributional properties of model-generated opinions and offer empirical evidence on the trade-offs between representativeness and accuracy in language modeling.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified value biases and evaluated distributions, directly optimizing for demographic representativeness as a core training objective, distinct from fairness or bias mitigation, is a novel formulation. It moves beyond post-hoc filtering or prompting, integrating sociological measurement directly into the optimization process.

proposals.title: Value-Aware Filtering: A Framework for Robust and Aligned Data Augmentation in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Back-translation is a powerful data augmentation technique, but its unsupervised nature risks amplifying errors and biases, corrupting the training distribution. This is a critical alignment problem, as low-quality synthetic data can degrade model performance and propagate undesirable values. Pre-2026 research by Durmus et al. on 'Values in the Wild' and the 'Reliability of Distribution Predictions' highlights the need for rigorous evaluation of model outputs and the real-world impact of noisy training data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-metric, error-aware filtering pipeline, integrated with a curriculum learning schedule, can selectively retain high-quality, value-consistent synthetic data from back-translation, leading to more robust and reliable model training than standard unfiltered augmentation.
proposals.method: We will implement a filtering pipeline using a suite of pre-trained models and metrics: NLI models for semantic consistency, GPT-2 perplexity for fluency, and domain classifiers for relevance. We will extend this with value-relevance scoring, inspired by Durmus's work, using classifiers trained on annotated datasets like ETHICS. A curriculum learning scheduler will weight the training loss, initially prioritizing real data and high-scoring synthetic examples, then gradually introducing lower-scoring, more challenging data. The entire system will be evaluated on downstream tasks and via human evaluations of output quality and alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, generalizable framework for safe data augmentation, directly addressing the noise problem in back-translation. It will contribute a new methodology for evaluating synthetic data quality that incorporates value consistency, bridging technical data augmentation research with AI alignment and values research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of value-aware metrics (beyond fluency/semantics) into the filtering pipeline and the combination of this multi-faceted filter with a curriculum learning strategy, creating a closed-loop system for robust augmentation informed by alignment concerns.

proposals.title: Evaluating Fidelity and Values in Cross-Lingual Procedural Knowledge Extraction from LLMs
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for procedural tasks, yet their knowledge can be unreliable or culturally biased, especially in non-English contexts. Pre-2026 research, including Durmus's work on values and economic tasks, highlights the need to audit the reliability and implicit values embedded in model outputs. Constructing high-quality, multilingual procedural datasets is crucial for training safer, more equitable models, but current methods lack systematic, automated quality assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: A system that uses cross-lingual alignment and multi-source agreement to score the fidelity of extracted procedural steps can produce a tiered dataset where higher-fidelity examples lead to more reliable and value-consistent model performance on cross-cultural how-to tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline using a strong multilingual LLM (e.g., Claude or GPT-4) to extract procedural steps from unstructured web sources in multiple languages. We will implement cross-lingual embedding alignment (using models like LaBSE) to cluster semantically equivalent steps. For each step, we will compute a composite fidelity score based on: 1) source credibility (via pre-2026 fact-checking datasets), 2) structural coherence (using sequence labeling models), and 3) cross-source/cross-lingual agreement. This scoring will enable the creation of a stratified dataset (high/medium/low fidelity).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable method for building audited multilingual procedural knowledge corpora. It will contribute to the field of AI reliability by introducing automated fidelity metrics for knowledge extraction and offer a dataset for analyzing how knowledge quality and implicit values vary across languages and sources.
proposals.novelty: While prior work evaluates LLM outputs or economic tasks, this proposal uniquely integrates cross-lingual alignment with a multi-faceted fidelity score to actively filter and tier training data, directly linking data quality to model reliability and value consistency in a multilingual setting.

proposals.title: Efficient Translation via Learned Routing: A Multi-Model System for Dynamic Cost-Quality Trade-offs
proposals.motivation: Large-scale deployment of machine translation (MT) is computationally expensive, yet many translation requests are for simple or repetitive sentences where a full-scale neural model is overkill. Current systems use a single, uniformly expensive model, ignoring the heterogeneity of translation difficulty and the availability of cheaper, specialized alternatives like phrase caches or smaller models. An adaptive system that dynamically routes sentences could drastically reduce inference costs for providers like Google Translate or Meta while maintaining quality, addressing a critical efficiency bottleneck in pre-2026 NLP.
proposals.hypothesis: An intelligent router, trained to predict translation difficulty and model suitability, can dynamically select from a portfolio of MT pathways (e.g., cache, statistical MT, small NMT, large transformer) to minimize computational cost per sentence while meeting a pre-defined quality threshold, making the overall system more efficient than any single model.
proposals.method: We will construct a portfolio of MT models, including a fast phrase cache, a small Marian-NMT model, and a large pre-2026 transformer (e.g., mBART). The core is a router model, likely a lightweight classifier (e.g., distilled BERT), trained on features like sentence length, perplexity, and semantic similarity to cached entries. It will be trained via reinforcement learning or imitation learning, using a reward function that penalizes computational cost (FLOPs, latency) and quality shortfalls (BLEURT, COMET) relative to a large-model baseline. The system will be evaluated on diverse datasets (e.g., FLORES, WMT) to measure aggregate cost savings and quality retention.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a framework for building cost-aware, heterogeneous MT systems, moving beyond static model selection. It will yield a publicly available benchmark for evaluating translation efficiency and a detailed analysis of which sentence attributes predict the viability of cheaper translation pathways, informing future model design.
proposals.novelty: While model cascades exist, this approach innovates by framing the problem as dynamic per-sentence resource allocation, directly analogous to energy-efficient computing architectures, and by rigorously learning the routing policy to optimize a formal cost-quality trade-off, rather than using handcrafted heuristics.

proposals.title: Evaluating Ecosystem Resilience in Cross-Lingual Summarization: A Framework for Robust, Inclusive Information Access
proposals.motivation: Cross-lingual summarization is critical for global information access, but current monolithic models are brittle. They are vulnerable to data poisoning, concept drift in low-resource languages, and adversarial attacks, which can sever crucial information flows. This fragility threatens the reliability of AI-assisted information distillation, especially for vulnerable linguistic communities, a core concern in the study of AI values in real-world use.
proposals.hypothesis: A cross-lingual summarization system designed as a diverse ensemble of agents—varying by architecture, training data, and translation pathway—will exhibit greater functional resilience (persistence and recovery of summary quality) under stress than a single state-of-the-art model, analogous to a robust ecological system.
proposals.method: We will construct an ecosystem of summarization agents, including: 1) fine-tuned mT5 and mBART models on different data slices, 2) pivot-based agents using different intermediary languages, and 3) a large multilingual LLM (e.g., Claude or GPT-4) as a baseline 'keystone' agent. We will simulate stressors like targeted noise injection (data poisoning), sudden domain shifts (concept drift), and adversarial prompts. Resilience will be measured via novel metrics tracking functional redundancy (performance overlap), recovery time after perturbation, and modularity's effect on failure containment, extending beyond standard ROUGE/BERTScore.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for evaluating and engineering AI systems for resilience, not just peak accuracy. It will yield concrete design principles for robust, linguistically inclusive NLP ecosystems and contribute a new evaluation suite for system-level robustness under stress, bridging ecological theory and AI system design.
proposals.novelty: While ensemble methods exist, applying ecological resilience principles (functional redundancy, adaptive cycles) to *system design* and *evaluation* for cross-lingual tasks is novel. It moves beyond benchmarking individual models to actively engineering and stress-testing a multi-agent ecosystem for persistent functionality, directly informed by the researcher's prior work on real-world AI values and reliability.

proposals.title: Auditing for Utility: A Tiered Framework for High-Quality, Multilingual Dataset Expansion
proposals.motivation: Current efforts to expand multilingual datasets like WikiLingua often prioritize language count over functional utility, risking the inclusion of low-quality or misaligned data that degrades cross-lingual model performance. This is a critical oversight for real-world applications, as seen in studies of economic tasks performed with AI, where reliable performance across languages is essential. A principled, audited expansion protocol is needed to ensure new data genuinely enhances model capabilities rather than introducing noise.
proposals.hypothesis: A tiered expansion protocol, which audits candidate languages for data availability, parallel corpus alignment quality, and linguistic distance, will produce a dataset where new 'verified-tier' languages yield significantly better cross-lingual transfer performance than languages added via naive, scale-first approaches.
proposals.method: We will design a three-phase audit: 1) Data Availability: Scrape and assess Wikipedia coverage for candidate languages. 2) Alignment Quality: Use sentence embeddings (e.g., LaBSE) and statistical measures (e.g., BLEU, chrF++) to score the alignment of candidate-language articles against English pivot articles from WikiLingua. 3) Linguistic Distance: Compute distances using URIEL typological features. Languages passing thresholds will be included in a new verified tier with detailed provenance metadata. We will then fine-tune a base multilingual model (e.g., mT5) on the original vs. tiered-expanded dataset and evaluate on true cross-lingual tasks like summarization and question-answering.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, replicable framework for quality-aware multilingual dataset curation, moving beyond simple scale-based metrics.
