It will yield a publicly released, tiered version of WikiLingua with clear confidence signals, enabling more reliable research into cross-lingual transfer. The audit methodology itself will be a contribution to evaluating the functional utility of multilingual data.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has analyzed values and reliability in LLM outputs, applying a rigorous, multi-faceted audit protocol to the dataset construction process—specifically for preventing quality degradation during expansion—is a novel, proactive intervention. It shifts the focus from ex-post model evaluation to ex-ante data quality assurance for multilingual systems.

proposals.title: Synthesizing Values: A Symbolic-Augmented Pipeline for Multilingual Task Data Generation in Low-Resource Contexts
proposals.motivation: Current multilingual LLMs exhibit significant performance disparities across languages, reflecting and potentially amplifying global digital inequities. This is particularly acute for tasks requiring nuanced understanding of cultural values, as existing datasets like WikiLingua lack coverage for many low-resource languages. End-to-end neural methods fail in these settings due to a near-total absence of parallel or monolingual training data, creating a critical bottleneck for building equitable, globally capable AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating non-differentiable, symbolic linguistic modules (e.g., morphological generators, syntactic rule engines) with a high-resource language LLM (like Claude) can synthesize grammatically coherent and task-relevant text in a low-resource target language, enabling the creation of usable training data where purely statistical methods cannot.
proposals.method: We will design a modular pipeline where an LLM (Claude) first decomposes high-resource language (HRL) seed sentences (e.g., from WikiLingua) into structured semantic representations and task-specific value frames, informed by Durmus's prior work on value discovery. Symbolic modules—leveraging existing linguistic resources like UniMorph for morphology, Apertium for shallow transfer, and bilingual lexicons—will then perform rule-based transformations to generate candidate target language (LRL) surface forms. A final validation module, potentially using a very small curated LRL corpus, will filter outputs for grammaticality and semantic fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable methodology for generating foundational task data for extremely low-resource languages, directly addressing a key technical barrier to multilingual AI equity. It will also yield a new synthetic dataset and a framework for analyzing how symbolic and neural components interact in hybrid NLP systems, contributing to both applied linguistics and AI methods.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses symbolic tools for analysis or post-hoc correction, this proposal uniquely positions them as core, generative components in a synthesis pipeline, explicitly bypassing the need for differentiable signal from the target language. This hybrid approach is distinct from prevailing paradigms of massive monolingual pretraining or few-shot cross-lingual transfer.

proposals.title: Adversarially Grounded Synthetic Data for Robust Cross-Lingual Transfer
proposals.motivation: Cross-lingual transfer for low-resource languages is bottlenecked by a lack of high-quality parallel data. While LLMs can generate synthetic data, prior work (e.g., on pseudo-distributions) shows such data often contains subtle artifacts and degenerates when used iteratively, failing to generalize to real-world tasks. This creates a critical need for a generation protocol that ensures synthetic data remains distributionally grounded and functionally useful.
proposals.hypothesis: A tripartite adversarial framework, which constrains synthetic data generation using signals for both realism (vs. authentic human data) and utility (on downstream tasks), will produce synthetic cross-lingual data that demonstrably improves model robustness and performance on external, real-world benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will implement a closed-loop system with three agents: a generator (a large multilingual LLM like Llama 2 or GPT-4), a discriminator (a model fine-tuned to distinguish real human translations from synthetic ones), and an evaluator (a task-specific model, e.g., for NLI or QA). The generator produces candidate synthetic sentence pairs. The discriminator provides a realism score, and the evaluator provides a utility score based on downstream task performance. These scores are combined into a reward for reinforcement learning (e.g., using PPO) to iteratively refine the generator's outputs, explicitly preventing distributional collapse.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, rigorously evaluated protocol for generating high-utility synthetic cross-lingual data. It will provide empirical evidence on how adversarial grounding mitigates synthetic data artifacts, advancing methodological rigor in data generation. The outcome is a practical framework for improving LLM performance in low-resource language settings, validated on external benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior synthetic data work, this integrates a dual adversarial signal (realism + utility) in a closed loop, directly addressing the self-reinforcing artifact problem identified in studies of LLM pseudo-distributions. It moves beyond single-metric generation to create data explicitly constrained by real-world distributions and task performance.

proposals.title: Crystallizing Cross-Lingual Data: Adversarial Refinement with Seed-Guided Feedback for Low-Resource Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: High-quality parallel data is scarce for most language pairs, limiting the performance of direct cross-lingual models. Current synthetic data generation methods often produce noisy or unnatural translations, which degrade model training. This work addresses the critical need for scalable, high-fidelity synthetic data generation to improve machine translation for low-resource languages.
proposals.hypothesis: A feedback loop, inspired by crystal growth, where an adversarial discriminator continuously critiques synthetic data against a small, high-quality seed set, will iteratively refine a generator to produce synthetic parallel data of significantly higher linguistic and semantic fidelity.
proposals.method: We will adapt a GAN-style framework with a generator (a large language model like Claude, prompted for translation) and a discriminator (a classifier trained on the seed data and negative examples). The discriminator will be trained to detect statistical and semantic 'impurities' (e.g., unnatural phrasing, semantic drift) in generated pairs. Its feedback, the 'crystallization signal', will be used to fine-tune the generator's parameters via reinforcement learning or gradient-based updates, creating an iterative refinement loop. The process begins with a small curated parallel corpus as the 'seed crystal'.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, principled method for generating high-quality synthetic cross-lingual data, directly improving direct translation models for low-resource pairs. It will also contribute a framework for evaluating the 'purity' of synthetic data, linking data quality to downstream model performance, extending research on the reliability of LLM predictions.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the structured, iterative 'crystallization' feedback loop, which moves beyond one-time filtering or simple prompting by using continuous adversarial discrimination against a gold-standard seed to guide data generation, a concept not explored in prior synthetic data work for NLP.

proposals.title: Architectural Diversity as a Diagnostic: Generating Targeted Synthetic Data for Robust Cross-Lingual Models
proposals.motivation: Current cross-lingual models often rely on monolithic architectures and synthetic data generated by similar models, leading to homogenization and shared failure modes across the ecosystem. This architectural convergence poses systemic risks, especially for low-resource languages, as it can amplify biases and create correlated errors. A method to systematically identify and address these architecture-specific weaknesses is needed to build more reliable and diverse multilingual AI.
proposals.hypothesis: By using a deliberately diverse set of model architectures (e.g., monolithic dense, modular, sparse) as diagnostic probes, we can generate targeted synthetic data that exposes and mitigates their distinct failure modes, leading to a more robust and heterogeneous ecosystem of cross-lingual models.
proposals.method: We will first establish a benchmark of diverse architectures (e.g., a dense Transformer, a Mixture-of-Experts model, and a modular system) on a suite of cross-lingual tasks. We will then analyze their failure patterns using methods from prior work on distribution predictions and real-world interactions. A feedback loop will be implemented where a large language model (e.g., Claude) is prompted to generate synthetic examples that specifically target the identified gaps of each architecture. This architecturally-aware synthetic corpus will be used to iteratively retrain and evaluate the models, measuring reductions in correlated errors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for generating higher-quality, diagnostic synthetic data that reduces architectural homogenization. It will yield a new benchmark for evaluating cross-lingual robustness and a heterogeneous model ecosystem with lower correlated failure rates, directly addressing ecosystem-wide risks identified in prior values and reliability research.
proposals.novelty: The core innovation is repurposing architectural diversity not just as an outcome, but as an active diagnostic tool to guide synthetic data generation, creating a closed-loop system that explicitly targets convergent weaknesses rather than generic 'quality'.

proposals.title: Valuing the Wild: A Human-in-the-Loop Framework for Value-Aligned Synthetic Data in Cross-Lingual Training
proposals.motivation: Direct cross-lingual models require massive, high-quality parallel or comparable data, which is scarce for most language pairs. Current synthetic data generation methods, often relying on LLM self-supervision, produce data of variable quality and may encode undesirable biases or misaligned values from the source model, as identified in pre-2026 studies of real-world LLM use. This limits the reliability and cultural appropriateness of models trained on such data.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating human evaluations of value alignment and task-specific quality into the synthetic data generation loop will produce training data that yields more reliable, value-consistent, and higher-performing direct cross-lingual models compared to data generated via purely automated methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel iterative framework where a strong LLM (e.g., Claude, as studied in the researcher's prior work) generates candidate cross-lingual data pairs for a target task. A human-in-the-loop evaluation module, inspired by 'Values in the Wild' methodologies, will assess these pairs on dimensions of factual reliability, cultural value alignment, and task fidelity. These human judgments will train a lightweight 'value critic' model to filter and score subsequent synthetic batches. The final curated dataset will train a direct cross-lingual model (e.g., a multilingual T5 or a custom encoder-decoder), with performance compared against baselines using automated metrics and human evaluations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide 1) a novel, scalable framework for generating higher-quality cross-lingual synthetic data, 2) new insights into how human value judgments can be operationalized for data curation, and 3) empirically validated direct cross-lingual models with demonstrably improved reliability and alignment on target tasks.
proposals.novelty: While synthetic data generation is well-studied, this proposal uniquely integrates the researcher's expertise in human evaluation of LLM values and real-world tasks directly into the data creation pipeline, moving beyond purely automated filtering or quality estimation metrics.

proposals.title: Provably Safe Data Augmentation: Formally Constrained Back-Translation for Robust Language Models
proposals.motivation: Foundation models exhibit unpredictable failures on safety-critical tasks, such as generating toxic or factually inconsistent content. Current data augmentation methods, like standard back-translation, increase linguistic diversity but do not provide guarantees that the synthetic data corrects specific failure modes. This gap necessitates a rigorous, verification-driven approach to data creation for safety alignment.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating formal verification with the back-translation data generation loop, we can produce synthetic training data that is provably constrained to satisfy specified safety properties, and that using this data for fine-tuning will yield models with provably improved robustness on those properties.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where a formal verifier (e.g., based on abstract interpretation or SMT solvers) defines a constrained space for a text generator (an LLM like Claude). The generator produces candidate text, which is back-translated via an unsupervised MT system. The formal verifier will check both the original and back-translated text against safety specifications (e.g., non-toxicity via a formal lexicon, logical consistency via knowledge graph grounding). Only data pairs satisfying the constraints are added to the augmentation set. This loop creates a 'safety filter' for data synthesis. The resulting dataset will be used to fine-tune a target model, and its robustness will be evaluated using formal and human evaluations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, formally-guided data augmentation pipeline for model safety, 2) a benchmark of formally specified safety properties for text, and 3) empirical evidence on the efficacy of verification-for-data-creation versus post-hoc verification alone, advancing the field towards provable AI safety.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the active use of formal methods to *guide* data generation for training, rather than only to audit a finished model, creating a synergistic bridge between formal verification and data-centric AI for safety.

proposals.title: Curriculum for Values: Adaptive Fine-Tuning of mBART to Elicit Culturally-Grounded Value Judgments
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) often encode values from their pretraining data, which may not align with specific cultural or community contexts, especially in low-resource languages. Fine-tuning on such niche, value-laden tasks risks catastrophic forgetting of the model's broad multilingual capabilities. A method is needed to specialize models for culturally-sensitive tasks without losing their general utility, addressing a core challenge in aligning LLMs with diverse human values.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a domain-specific curriculum learning strategy, which progressively fine-tunes mBART from general multilingual tasks to complex, culturally-grounded value elicitation tasks, will yield models that are more reliable and aligned for target cultures while better preserving their original multilingual knowledge compared to standard direct fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will adaptively fine-tune mBART-large-50. Starting from high-resource language tasks (e.g., NLI), we will design a curriculum of increasing difficulty and specificity: 1) multilingual factual QA, 2) cross-lingual sentiment/emotion detection, 3) value-statement classification using translated datasets like Moral Foundations, and finally 4) generation of culturally-contextualized value judgments on low-resource language prompts. We will use a task-adaptive scheduler to transition based on validation loss plateaus, employing a modified Elastic Weight Consolidation loss to constrain catastrophic forgetting.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical, neuroscience-inspired fine-tuning framework for value alignment in multilingual settings. It will provide empirical evidence on how curriculum structure affects both task performance and knowledge retention in LLMs, offering a new methodology for researchers and practitioners aiming to adapt models to specific cultural value systems.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning exists in NLP, its application to the problem of value alignment and cultural adaptation of a multilingual model like mBART is novel. This approach uniquely bridges computational neuroscience principles with the goal of preserving a model's broad capabilities while specializing it for ethically-sensitive, low-resource tasks.

proposals.title: Cross-Cultural Value Alignment: Probing and Steering Multilingual Pretrained Models
proposals.motivation: Prior work by Durmus et al. (e.g., 'Values in the Wild') has shown that LLMs express values in real-world interactions, but this analysis is largely English-centric. As AI is deployed globally, understanding and aligning the implicit values embedded in multilingual models is critical for safe, equitable deployment across diverse cultural contexts. This problem is urgent given the widespread use of models like mBART and the lack of systematic frameworks for evaluating their cross-cultural value representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that large-scale multilingual pretrained models (e.g., mBART, XLM-R) encode distinct, culturally-situated value priors across languages, and that these priors can be systematically measured and steered via targeted fine-tuning on value-annotated multilingual data.
proposals.method: We will use mBART-50 as a base model. First, we will develop a multilingual diagnostic dataset by translating and culturally adapting value-laden prompts from prior work (e.g., the Moral Foundations Questionnaire) into 10+ languages. We will then probe the model's generations across languages using human evaluations and automated metrics to map its value 'pseudo-distributions' (extending methods from Durmus's reliability work). Finally, we will experiment with parameter-efficient fine-tuning (e.g., LoRA) on a curated, balanced multilingual dataset to steer value expressions while preserving linguistic capabilities.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel diagnostic framework and dataset for auditing cross-cultural value representations in multilingual models, 2) empirical findings on the relationship between pretraining language data and embedded values, and 3) a proof-of-concept method for culturally-aware value alignment that does not require full retraining.
proposals.novelty: While value alignment is studied in English LLMs, systematically diagnosing and steering the culturally-situated values within a single, shared multilingual model's parameters is novel. It bridges Durmus's research on values-in-the-wild and AI task analysis with the technical paradigm of multilingual model adaptation.

proposals.title: Cross-Lingual Semantic Alignment via Contrastive Learning: Evaluating Value Consistency in Summarization
proposals.motivation: Multilingual summarization is crucial for global information access, but ensuring that summaries preserve the core values and semantic intent of source articles across languages remains an open challenge. Pre-2026 work, including Durmus's research on values in real-world LLM interactions, highlights the importance of value-aligned AI, yet methods for evaluating and enforcing cross-lingual semantic and value consistency are underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a contrastive learning objective, which pulls together the representations of a source article and its faithful summary while pushing apart mismatched pairs, can create a shared embedding space where semantically and value-aligned content clusters across languages, enabling more robust automatic evaluation of cross-lingual summary quality.
proposals.method: We will fine-tune a multilingual encoder (e.g., mBERT or XLM-R) using a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE). Positive pairs will be (source article, high-quality human-written summary) in multiple languages, drawn from datasets like XL-Sum. Negative pairs will be constructed by pairing articles with irrelevant or value-distorted summaries. We will evaluate alignment using both intrinsic metrics (e.g., clustering quality) and extrinsic tasks, such as training a classifier on this space to detect value inconsistencies, inspired by Durmus's evaluation methodologies for real-world AI tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel method and benchmark for assessing cross-lingual semantic and value alignment in text generation. It extends the study of values in AI to the multilingual domain and provides a more nuanced, representation-based tool for evaluating summarization systems beyond surface-level metrics like ROUGE.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is established in vision and monolingual NLP, its application to explicitly align value-laden semantic content across languages for summarization evaluation is novel. It bridges Durmus's focus on values and real-world tasks with advanced representation learning techniques to address a core multilingual AI reliability problem.

proposals.title: Adapting Synthetic Voices: A Value-Aware, Few-Shot Framework for Low-Resource Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: High-quality machine translation (MT) remains inaccessible for many low-resource languages due to a critical scarcity of aligned, real-world parallel data. While large-scale synthetic data generation via LLMs offers a potential solution, its distributional mismatch with real language use—encompassing stylistic, cultural, and pragmatic values—limits its direct utility. This work addresses the core challenge of adapting vast, but potentially biased or unnatural synthetic corpora to a target domain using only a handful of authentic examples, a scenario reflective of real-world constraints for underserved languages.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a few-shot adaptation function, explicitly trained to map the stylistic and pragmatic 'values' embedded in synthetic text to those in real human translations, can effectively bridge the domain gap, enabling performant MT models by leveraging the adapted synthetic data for training.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, building on Esin Durmus's work on analyzing values in LLM interactions, we will design a lightweight 'value probe' to characterize the distributional differences (e.g., formality, hedging, cultural specificity) between synthetic and real parallel sentences. Second, we will train a small, parameter-efficient adapter network (e.g., a series of low-rank adaptation (LoRA) layers) on the few real examples to transform the synthetic source sentences, minimizing the divergence measured by the value probe and a reconstruction loss. This adapted synthetic corpus will then be used to fine-tune a base multilingual MT model (e.g., mBART).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, value-aware methodology for few-shot synthetic-to-real domain adaptation in MT, 2) a rigorous evaluation framework assessing both fluency/accuracy and value alignment in the adapted outputs, and 3) empirical evidence on the feasibility of building MT systems with minimal real data, directly impacting low-resource language preservation efforts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly framing the synthetic-to-real gap as a 'values' alignment problem and learning a direct adaptation function on this axis, rather than merely fine-tuning the end MT model on a mixed corpus, which is less data-efficient and fails to correct systematic synthetic biases.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning for Low-Resource Cross-Lingual Transfer: A Framework for Few-Shot Adaptation to Unseen Language Pairs
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit significant performance disparities across languages, with low-resource languages suffering from a lack of aligned data for effective cross-lingual transfer. This inequity limits the global applicability and fairness of AI systems. Current few-shot prompting methods are brittle, and standard fine-tuning requires substantial parallel data, which is unavailable for thousands of language pairs.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning framework, pre-trained on a diverse set of existing low-resource translation tasks, can learn a general parameter initialization that enables rapid adaptation to a new, unseen language pair with only a few aligned examples.
proposals.method: We will implement a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework, adapting its established use in few-shot image classification and drug discovery. The model, a standard encoder-decoder transformer, will be meta-trained on a constructed suite of hundreds of diverse, small-scale bilingual translation tasks (e.g., from OPUS). Each meta-task simulates a few-shot learning scenario. The outer optimization loop will learn initialization parameters that are sensitive to gradient updates from minimal data, enabling fast adaptation via a few gradient steps on a held-out, novel language pair during meta-testing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, model-based solution for few-shot cross-lingual transfer, moving beyond prompt engineering. It will yield a systematic evaluation of meta-learning's efficacy for language adaptation and offer insights into the 'learnability' of language representations from minimal data, contributing to the study of LLM capabilities and values like equity.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning is established in vision and biology, its application to few-shot cross-lingual transfer for unseen language pairs is novel. This adapts a proven methodological paradigm from computational biology to a core, unsolved challenge in NLP, creating a new bridge between fields.

proposals.title: Few-Shot Value Alignment Across Languages: Measuring and Mitigating Cross-Cultural Transfer Gaps
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are deployed globally, but their alignment is often optimized for high-resource languages, risking value misalignment and poor performance in low-resource contexts. Current fine-tuning methods require substantial, curated data per language, which is infeasible for the vast majority of the world's languages. This work addresses the critical need for efficient, few-shot alignment methods that can generalize values and tasks across language pairs with minimal examples.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, contrastive few-shot learning approach, leveraging meta-learning and value-aware prompting, can significantly improve cross-lingual value alignment and task performance compared to standard few-shot prompting or direct translation, even with only 3-5 aligned examples per target language.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage method. First, we will adapt the evaluation framework from 'Values in the Wild' to create a diagnostic benchmark of value-sensitive tasks (e.g., fairness, harm prevention) across diverse language pairs. Second, we will design a meta-prompting algorithm that uses a small 'alignment seed set' for a target language to condition a base model (like Claude). This algorithm will employ in-context learning with contrastive examples of aligned vs. misaligned responses, guided by a value taxonomy, to steer model behavior. We will rigorously evaluate against baselines including direct translation of prompts and zero-shot cross-lingual transfer.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel benchmark for evaluating few-shot cross-lingual value alignment, 2) a practical, lightweight algorithm for adapting LLMs to new languages with minimal aligned data, and 3) empirical insights into which values and tasks transfer most robustly, extending analysis from 'Which Economic Tasks are Performed with AI?' to the alignment domain.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied values in LLM interactions or economic tasks separately, our approach uniquely combines these themes to tackle the specific, under-explored problem of data-efficient cross-lingual value transfer, moving beyond English-centric alignment evaluations.

proposals.title: Active Preference Learning for Adaptive and Context-Aware Document-Level MT Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Evaluating document-level machine translation (MT) quality remains a significant challenge, as static benchmarks fail to capture the nuanced, context-dependent decisions required for coherent discourse and genre-appropriate style. Current evaluation methods, often based on sentence-level metrics or static human-annotated datasets, are poorly aligned with real-world translation tasks where quality is holistic and multidimensional. This misalignment hinders the development and reliable assessment of document-level MT systems.
proposals.hypothesis: An active learning framework that strategically queries human preferences for challenging, discourse-rich translation examples will produce a more robust, adaptive, and context-aware evaluation model than one trained on a static, passively collected dataset.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-model system: a preference prediction model (e.g., a fine-tuned LLM like Claude or a specialized transformer) and an acquisition function for active learning. The predictor, initially trained on existing document-level preference data (e.g., from MQM frameworks), will score candidate translation pairs. Using an uncertainty sampling strategy (e.g., based on prediction entropy or committee disagreement), the system will select the most informative examples for human evaluation, explicitly targeting discourse phenomena like coherence, anaphora resolution, and genre conventions. These new human judgments will iteratively refine the predictor and expand the benchmark dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, scalable protocol for building dynamic document-level MT evaluation benchmarks, 2) an analysis of which translation phenomena are most challenging for automated evaluators and thus most valuable for human annotation, and 3) a publicly released adaptive evaluation model and a growing dataset of high-value human preferences, directly informing both evaluation research and MT system development.
proposals.novelty: While active learning is established, its application to creating adaptive *evaluation benchmarks* for document-level MT is novel. This moves beyond using active learning to improve MT systems themselves, instead focusing on making the evaluation framework itself a learnable, strategic process that targets the core weaknesses of current automated metrics.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Coherence: A Multi-Scale Framework for Diagnosing Document-Level Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: Current machine translation (MT) evaluation is dominated by sentence-level metrics like BLEU, which fail to capture document-level qualities such as narrative flow, entity consistency, and pragmatic coherence. As LLMs are increasingly used for real-world, long-form translation tasks, there is a critical need for evaluation frameworks that can diagnose where and how document-level translation failures occur. This gap limits our ability to improve models for tasks requiring cross-sentence understanding and coherence.
proposals.hypothesis: Document-level translation quality can be modeled as a scaling phenomenon, where local errors at the sentence or paragraph level propagate and compound in predictable ways to degrade higher-level discourse properties. By analyzing translation outputs across a hierarchy of linguistic scales, we can develop diagnostic metrics that identify the primary loci of coherence breakdown.
proposals.method: We will adapt the multi-scale analysis framework from structural geology, treating a translated document as a composite material. Using aligned document-level datasets (e.g., from WMT or IWSLT), we will first annotate errors and coherence breaks at multiple scales: lexical/syntactic (sentence), discourse (paragraph), and narrative (section/document). We will then train statistical models (e.g., hierarchical Bayesian models or graph neural networks) to predict document-level human quality judgments from these multi-scale error signatures. The core technical innovation is deriving 'scaling laws' that quantify how error types at one level affect coherence at higher levels.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, diagnostic evaluation framework for document-level MT that moves beyond a single score to explain *why* a translation fails at the document level. It will contribute a new dataset of multi-scale coherence annotations and a set of interpretable metrics that can guide the development of more coherent, long-form translation models. This aligns with Durmus's research on evaluating AI in real-world tasks and understanding model failures.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual analogy to scale-effect problems in material science and its application to linguistic coherence. Unlike existing work that treats document-level evaluation as a monolithic task, this framework explicitly models the hierarchical propagation of errors, offering a principled, diagnostic alternative.

proposals.title: Evaluating Coherence and Value Consistency in Document-Level Machine Translation with Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: While LLMs have revolutionized machine translation (MT), standard sentence-level evaluations fail to capture document-level qualities like discourse coherence, pronoun consistency, and the preservation of nuanced values across long texts. As LLMs are increasingly used for translating complex documents (e.g., literature, legal texts, user manuals), there is a critical need for evaluation frameworks that assess these higher-level properties, aligning with research on how AI is used for complex economic tasks and the values embedded in real-world LLM interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging a large-scale, aligned dataset of human-LLM interactions, we can develop a robust evaluation framework that reveals significant, systematic differences between LLMs and traditional MT systems in their ability to maintain document-level coherence and value consistency, differences that are invisible to sentence-level metrics like BLEU.
proposals.method: We will adapt the methodology from 'Values in the Wild' and 'Which Economic Tasks are Performed with AI?' to construct a document-level MT evaluation dataset. Using a subset of the Claude conversation corpus, we will identify and extract multi-turn, document-length translation tasks. We will then evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs (e.g., GPT-4, Claude 2) and specialized MT systems on this dataset, employing a mixed-methods approach: automated metrics for lexical cohesion and entity tracking, and structured human evaluations (via platforms like Prolific) focused on coherence, style consistency, and value preservation (e.g., tone, formality, cultural nuance). Statistical analysis will correlate human judgments with automated scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, publicly available benchmark for document-level MT evaluation grounded in real-world usage data, 2) a systematic analysis of LLM capabilities and failures in long-form translation, and 3) new insights into the 'reliability' of LLM outputs at the discourse level, extending research on distributional predictions to the domain of cross-lingual text generation.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely bridges the study of values and real-world LLM use with core NLP evaluation, moving beyond synthetic or sentence-level benchmarks to assess translation quality on authentic, document-length tasks sourced from millions of user interactions, a perspective largely absent in pre-2026 MT research.

proposals.title: Genre-Aware Summarization via Domain-Specific Feature Engineering: A Bioinformatic Analogy for Legal, Scientific, and News Text
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) often fail to generalize reliably across specialized text genres, as seen in real-world interactions where generic models produce suboptimal or value-misaligned outputs. Current fine-tuning approaches are data-hungry and lack a principled understanding of genre-specific structural and functional features, analogous to the challenge in bioinformatics of transferring models between organisms. This research addresses the need for robust, interpretable adaptation of summarization models to new domains without massive labeled datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly engineering and incorporating domain-defining structural and functional features (e.g., IMRaD sections, legal citation patterns) into a summarization model's architecture or training objective, we can achieve more effective and reliable generalization to new text genres than with direct application or standard fine-tuning of a generic LLM.
proposals.method: We will first define feature extractors for target genres (legal, scientific, news) using rule-based and learned methods (e.g., spaCy patterns for legal citations, a BERT-based classifier for IMRaD section labels). For a base summarization model (e.g., BART or T5), we will integrate these features via concatenated embeddings or auxiliary training objectives that predict genre features. We will evaluate using human and automated metrics on summarization tasks, comparing our genre-adapted model against a generic baseline and a standard fine-tuned model, analyzing performance and reliability of distribution predictions across genres.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for data-efficient domain adaptation in NLP, inspired by bioinformatics. It will yield interpretable, genre-aware summarization models and a curated set of domain-specific features for legal, scientific, and news text. The findings will advance understanding of how structural priors improve LLM reliability and generalization in real-world tasks.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct analogy to feature engineering in bioinformatics for model transfer, moving beyond generic embeddings or prompt engineering. It explicitly models genre as a set of conserved functional features, a perspective not fully explored in pre-2026 NLP adaptation work.

ideas.title: Ecological Resilience for Cross-Lingual Summarization Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research applies ecological resilience principles—such as functional redundancy, modularity, and adaptive cycles—to the design of AI ecosystems for low-to-high-resource language summarization. Instead of a single model, the system employs a diverse ensemble of summarization agents (e.g., different architectures, training data subsets, or pivot-language pathways) that can compensate for each other's failures, much like species in an ecosystem. This approach ensures continuous, reliable information distillation from vulnerable low-resource language sources into high-resource summaries, even when individual models are compromised by data poisoning, concept drift, or targeted adversarial attacks. The resulting ecosystem is evaluated not just on accuracy, but on metrics of functional persistence and recovery, creating summarization systems that are both linguistically inclusive and inherently robust.

ideas.title: Active Preference Learning for Adaptive Document-Level Translation Evaluation
ideas.description: This research develops an adaptive evaluation framework for document-level machine translation that moves beyond a static aligned dataset. It employs an active learning loop where a preference prediction model, initially trained on existing document pairs, identifies the most informative new translation examples for human assessment. By strategically collecting human judgments on these challenging cases—focusing on discourse phenomena, genre-specific conventions, and explicit quality dimensions—the system iteratively refines both its evaluation model and the underlying benchmark. This results in a robust, scalable, and context-aware evaluation protocol that directly captures the nuanced decisions inherent in document-level translation quality.

ideas.title: Architecturally-Diverse Synthetic Data for Cross-Lingual Model Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel feedback loop where synthetic data generation for cross-lingual tasks is guided by the performance gaps and failure modes of a deliberately diverse set of model architectures (e.g., modular, monolithic, sparse, dense). Instead of generating generic 'high-quality' data, the system produces targeted synthetic examples that challenge specific architectural biases, creating a more robust and comprehensive training corpus. This architecturally-aware data generation, in turn, is used to train and benchmark a new generation of heterogeneous cross-lingual models, reducing ecosystem-wide risks from homogenization. The core innovation is using architectural diversity as both a driver for higher-quality synthetic data creation and as a safeguard against convergent failure modes in multilingual AI.

ideas.title: Use crowdsourcing quality control methods for human evaluation
ideas.description: Adapt techniques from crowdsourcing research to improve consistency of human judgments in NLG.

ideas.title: Improve question generation for FEQA

ideas.title: Adversarially Grounded Synthetic Data for Robust Cross-Lingual Transfer
ideas.description: This research develops a closed-loop system for generating synthetic cross-lingual training data that is explicitly constrained and refined by adversarial feedback from real data distributions. The core innovation is a tripartite framework where a generator model produces candidate synthetic pairs, a discriminator model trained on authentic human translations provides a signal for realism, and a downstream task evaluator provides a signal for functional utility. This adversarial grounding ensures the synthetic data avoids degenerating into a self-reinforcing, artifact-laden distribution, forcing it to remain aligned with the complexities of genuine language use. The outcome is a robust data generation protocol that produces synthetic data proven to enhance model performance on external, real-world benchmarks, not just internal synthetic tests.

ideas.title: Adaptive Multi-Model Routing for Efficient Machine Translation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that translation must use a single, uniformly expensive model at inference. By drawing an analogy from computer architecture's use of heterogeneous cores and dynamic scheduling for energy efficiency, we propose creating a highly efficient translation system via adaptive multi-model routing. The analogous solution uses a dispatcher to match task demand to appropriate core types (big/little). Adapted to machine translation, an intelligent router would analyze each input sentence's attributes and select from a portfolio of translation pathways—such as a fast statistical model, a small neural model, a cached phrase bank, or a large state-of-the-art transformer—to minimize computational cost while meeting a quality threshold. This makes a pipeline approach competitive by optimizing the cost-quality trade-off dynamically per sentence, rather than statically for the entire workload.

ideas.title: Extend analysis to more LLMs and languages
ideas.description: Apply the Marked Personas method to a broader set of language models and non-English languages.

ideas.description: Compare predicate-argument structures from the summary and source to detect inconsistencies.
ideas.sourceTitle: FEQA: A Question Answering Evaluation Framework for Faithfulness Assessment in Abstractive Summarization

ideas.title: Few-Shot Synthetic-to-Real Domain Adaptation for Low-Resource Machine Translation
ideas.description: This research direction develops novel domain adaptation techniques specifically designed for the extreme low-data regime of few-shot learning. The core challenge is to bridge the significant distribution gap between large-scale synthetic parallel corpora and a tiny set of high-quality, real aligned examples for a target language pair. The method will focus on learning a robust adaptation function from the synthetic to the real domain using the few examples, which then allows the model to effectively leverage the vast amounts of synthetic data for the target task. This creates a synergistic loop where the few real examples provide the critical signal for adaptation, and the adapted synthetic data provides the volume needed for stable learning, enabling performant machine translation for languages where only minimal aligned data exists.

ideas.title: Document-level MT evaluation using multi-scale coherence metrics
ideas.description: This research adapts the multi-scale analysis framework from structural geology and rock mechanics to the problem of evaluating document-level machine translation. In rock mechanics, the 'scale effect' problem is solved by testing material properties at multiple scales (micro to macro) and developing statistical scaling laws that predict how micro-scale features (like grain boundaries and micro-fractures) affect macro-scale structural behavior. Adapted to MT evaluation, this approach would use the aligned document-level dataset to analyze translation quality not just at the document level, but across a hierarchy of linguistic scales: sentence, paragraph, section, and full document. The research would develop novel metrics that quantify how local translation errors (lexical, syntactic) propagate and compound across these scales to affect higher-level document properties like coherence, narrative flow, and pragmatic consistency. This multi-scale evaluation framework would provide more granular diagnostic information than single-level document metrics, revealing whether document-level translation failures originate primarily at sentence boundaries, paragraph transitions, or broader discourse levels.

ideas.title: Relax the need for a single best summary
ideas.description: Develop evaluation frameworks that accommodate multiple valid summary styles, both human and LLM.
ideas.sourceTitle: Benchmarking Large Language Models for News Summarization

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Generate evaluation metrics from model outputs
ideas.description: Automatically discover or learn evaluation criteria by analyzing distributions of high-quality model outputs.

ideas.title: Dual: Measure human similarity to model opinions

ideas.title: Symbolic-Augmented Multilingual Data Synthesis for Non-Differentiable Language Expansion
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel method for expanding multilingual datasets like WikiLingua to include languages with extremely scarce resources. Instead of relying solely on end-to-end differentiable models for translation or data generation, the approach integrates symbolic reasoning modules—such as morphological analyzers, syntactic templates, and bilingual dictionaries—to programmatically generate or validate training examples. This hybrid system can synthesize coherent, grammatically correct text in a target language by applying non-differentiable symbolic transformations to seed data from higher-resource languages. The resulting pipeline allows for the scalable and controlled expansion of multilingual capabilities in foundation models, even for languages where differentiable statistical methods fail due to data paucity.

ideas.title: Econometric Valuation of Foundation Models Across a Multilingual Dataset Spectrum
ideas.description: This research direction expands a high-quality, structured dataset like WikiLingua to a broad and diverse set of languages, creating a controlled multilingual testbed. It then applies econometric models (e.g., difference-in-differences, instrumental variables) to this dataset to measure how improvements in a model's multilingual capability—such as adding support for a new language—affect real-world economic outcomes like productivity, information access equity, and knowledge creation. The synergy produces a rigorous, data-driven methodology for valuing the 'language dividend' of AI development, informing both resource allocation for model training and policy for equitable digital public goods.

ideas.title: Extend framework to more models and languages

ideas.title: Domain-Specific Immunization for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research adapts the principle of targeted immunization from immunology to challenge the assumption that broad, general-purpose training data is optimal. In vaccine development, a specific antigen trains the immune system for potent, specialized defense. Analogously, we propose constructing a 'core antigenic dataset'—a highly curated, narrow-domain corpus that encapsulates the fundamental syntactic, semantic, and logical structures of a specialty (e.g., legal reasoning, medical diagnostics). The foundation model undergoes intensive 'immunization' on this core set, followed by strategic 'booster' training on adversarial or rare edge cases within the same domain. The hypothesis is that this focused, antigen-like training will produce a model with superior, more robust, and deeply internalized expertise for its specialized task than one trained on a vast but diluted general corpus, potentially improving efficiency, accuracy, and reasoning fidelity within the target domain.

ideas.title: Few-shot cross-lingual transfer via meta-learning from low-resource biology
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational biology, where meta-learning is used for drug discovery with minimal data for rare disease targets. The analogous solution involves pre-training on many small, distinct compound-activity tasks to learn a general model initialization that can quickly adapt to new targets with few examples. Adapted to the original idea of scaling down to few-shot learning for language pairs, we propose developing a meta-learning framework for cross-lingual transfer. The method would pre-train on a wide variety of existing, low-resource translation tasks to learn a general parameter initialization. This meta-initialized model could then be rapidly fine-tuned with only a few aligned examples for a new, unseen language pair, effectively solving the few-shot learning challenge by leveraging prior experience across many language adaptation tasks.

ideas.title: Cross-lingual Procedural Knowledge Extraction with Automated Fidelity Scoring
ideas.description: This system constructs high-quality multilingual how-to datasets by actively filtering and validating content from diverse, unstructured sources. It employs cross-lingual alignment to identify consistent procedural steps across languages and sources, boosting signal. Each data point receives a fidelity score based on source credibility, structural coherence, and agreement with other sources, allowing for the creation of tiered datasets. The final output is a robust, quality-controlled corpus suitable for training models on reliable, cross-cultural procedural knowledge.

ideas.title: Scale down: influence for small, efficient models
ideas.description: Apply the scaled-up EK-FAC method to study generalization in small, on-device language models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Studying Large Language Model Generalization with Influence Functions

ideas.title: Apply Domain-Specific Feature Engineering from Bioinformatics to Text Genre Adaptation
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from bioinformatics, where models are transferred between biological domains not by direct application but through domain-specific feature engineering. The analogous problem is applying a model trained on one organism's genome to another's, solved by identifying conserved functional features (like codon bias or motifs) and adapting the model using them. For direct summarization, we propose to first engineer a set of defining features for new target genres (e.g., legal discourse: precedent citations, holding/rationale structure; scientific papers: IMRaD structure, citation function; news: inverted pyramid, named entity chains). The core summarization method would then be adapted by incorporating or retraining on these genre-specific feature extractors, enabling effective generalization beyond how-to articles by building genre-aware models rather than applying a generic model directly.

ideas.title: Static benchmarks capture dynamic model behavior
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that a fixed benchmark adequately measures models that learn from user interactions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Tiered, Quality-Audited Expansion of WikiLingua for High-Utility Multilingual Coverage
ideas.description: This redesigned system addresses the quality degradation risk of naive expansion by establishing a multi-phase audit protocol for candidate languages. It first evaluates data availability, parallel corpus alignment quality, and linguistic distance from existing languages. Only languages passing a minimum quality threshold are included in a new, verified tier of the dataset, with clear metadata on provenance and confidence. This approach ensures the expanded dataset improves model performance on true cross-lingual transfer tasks rather than simply increasing the language count with noisy or ineffective data.

ideas.title: Scale down to few-shot learning
ideas.description: Develop methods that work with only a few aligned examples per language pair.
ideas.sourceTitle: WikiLingua: A New Benchmark Dataset for Multilingual Abstractive Summarization

ideas.title: Synthetic Data Refinement via Adversarial Discriminators and Crystallization Feedback
ideas.description: This idea proposes improving synthetic data generation for cross-lingual models by drawing an analogy from materials science, specifically the controlled growth of high-purity single crystals. The analogous solution involves using a seed crystal within a supersaturated solution, where the growth process inherently rejects impurities and is guided by real-time structural feedback to ensure perfection. Adapted to NLP, the method uses a small curated set of genuine parallel data as a seed. A synthetic data generator produces candidate cross-lingual pairs, which are then scrutinized by an adversarial discriminator trained to detect statistical and semantic 'impurities' relative to the seed distribution. This discriminator's output provides a continuous feedback signal to refine the generator's parameters, creating an iterative 'crystallization' loop that progressively improves the structural coherence and linguistic fidelity of the synthetic training data, thereby enhancing the quality of the resulting direct cross-lingual models.

ideas.title: Relax the need for explicit answer matching
ideas.description: Develop metrics that detect faithfulness via entailment or paraphrasing, not just string matching.
ideas.sourceTitle: FEQA: A Question Answering Evaluation Framework for Faithfulness Assessment in Abstractive Summarization

ideas.title: Adopt adversarial debiasing from NLP fairness
ideas.description: Apply gradient-based adversarial training techniques from language models to reduce biases in vision generators.
ideas.sourceTitle: Easily Accessible Text-to-Image Generation Amplifies Demographic Stereotypes at Large Scale

ideas.title: Error-Aware Filtered Back-Translation with Curriculum Learning for Robust Data Augmentation
ideas.description: This system applies unsupervised back-translation for data augmentation but directly addresses its noise-amplification flaw by implementing a robust quality-filtering pipeline. Synthetic examples are scored using multiple metrics—including semantic consistency, fluency, and domain relevance—and only those surpassing a dynamic threshold are retained for training. The model is trained using a curriculum that prioritizes real data and high-confidence synthetic data early on, progressively incorporating more challenging examples. This approach ensures the benefits of scale from back-translation while safeguarding against the corruption of the training distribution by low-quality, erroneous text.

ideas.title: Adaptive Fine-Tuning of mBART Using Domain-Specific Curriculum Learning
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience and motor skill learning, where curriculum learning—progressively increasing task difficulty—is key to robust skill acquisition, this idea adapts that principle to fine-tuning multilingual pretrained models like mBART. The analogous solution involves structuring training from simplified to complex task variations to build stable neural representations. Adapted to this NLP context, the research idea is to perform adaptive fine-tuning of mBART using a domain-specific curriculum. Instead of direct fine-tuning on the final low-resource or complex task, training would begin on a simplified proxy (e.g., a related high-resource language task, a general-domain version, or a task with shorter input lengths) and systematically progress through increasingly difficult or specific subtasks until reaching the target objective. This method aims to prevent catastrophic forgetting of the model's broad multilingual knowledge while more effectively specializing it for the end application.

ideas.title: Dual: use summaries to evaluate pretraining data
ideas.description: Use summary quality as a diagnostic tool to evaluate the composition and quality of a model's pretraining data.
ideas.sourceTitle: Benchmarking Large Language Models for News Summarization

ideas.title: Investigate causes of representational biases

ideas.sourceTitle: Towards Measuring the Representation of Subjective Global Opinions in Language Models

ideas.title: Formally Verified Few-Shot Safety Alignment for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research direction develops methods to apply formal verification to the few-shot learning process of large foundation models. The core challenge is to design a verification framework that can analyze a model's internal mechanisms and prove that specific safety properties (e.g., non-toxicity, factual consistency, refusal of harmful instructions) will be preserved when the model is adapted using only a few aligned examples per new task or language pair. This moves beyond post-hoc auditing to enable provably safe in-context learning and parameter-efficient fine-tuning. The synergy creates a new paradigm where models can be rapidly and safely specialized for low-resource scenarios with mathematically assured alignment, drastically reducing the data and computational overhead typically required for robust safety measures.

ideas.title: A single 'global' model is the target
ideas.description: Challenge the goal of creating a single model that equitably represents all global opinions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Towards Measuring the Representation of Subjective Global Opinions in Language Models

ideas.title: Formally Verified Back-Translation for Provable Robustness in Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research direction integrates back-translation from unsupervised MT with formal verification to create a rigorous data augmentation framework for foundation model safety. The core idea is to use formal specifications of desired safety properties (e.g., non-toxicity, factual consistency) to constrain the back-translation process, generating synthetic data that is both linguistically diverse and formally guaranteed to satisfy the specified constraints. This formally verified synthetic data is then used to retrain or fine-tune the model, providing provable guarantees that the model's behavior on the augmented data distribution adheres to the safety properties. The synergy lies in using formal methods not just to audit a static model, but to actively guide the creation of training data that systematically and provably corrects or hardens the model against specified failure modes.

ideas.title: Cross-lingual alignment via molecular docking-inspired contrastive learning
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational biology—specifically, the molecular docking problem of finding complementary binding poses between molecules—to the task of cross-lingual article-summary alignment. In docking, contrastive learning is implicitly used to distinguish correct, geometrically complementary complexes from decoys. We adapt this by framing article-summary pairs as 'molecular complexes' where a correct pair has high representational complementarity. A contrastive learning model will be trained to pull together the vector representations of an article and its true summary (across any language) in a shared embedding space, while pushing apart representations of 'decoy' complexes (non-matching articles and summaries). This forces the model to learn abstract, language-invariant features of content correspondence, much like a docking algorithm learns the abstract principle of shape and chemical complementarity, enabling robust alignment between articles and summaries across different languages.

ideas.title: Theoretical Framework for Emergent Multilingualism in Cross-Lingual Transfer
ideas.description: This research proposes to develop a theoretical framework that explains and predicts emergent capabilities specifically within the context of multilingual pretrained models like mBART. By systematically scaling model size and data across diverse languages, the project will test hypotheses about how linguistic typology and cross-lingual alignment mediate the emergence of new abilities. The goal is to move beyond observing scale-driven emergence in a single language to creating a predictive theory of 'emergent multilingual transfer,' identifying the conditions under which abstract reasoning capabilities manifest universally versus language-specifically.

ideas.title: Improve gradient computation for candidate sequences
ideas.description: Develop more efficient techniques beyond TF-IDF filtering and batching for computing training sequence gradients.
ideas.sourceTitle: Studying Large Language Model Generalization with Influence Functions

ideas.title: A Phylogenetic Sampling Strategy for Multilingual Dataset Expansion
ideas.description: This idea adapts the phylogenetic sampling strategy from evolutionary biology to the problem of expanding WikiLingua beyond its initial 18 languages. In biology, to efficiently capture the diversity of a trait, researchers sample representative species from across a phylogenetic tree. Analogously, for dataset expansion, we would construct or use an existing language phylogeny to identify major language families and sub-families not yet represented. The solution is to then strategically select new languages that fill these high-level phylogenetic gaps (e.g., adding a Dravidian, Afro-Asiatic, or Niger-Congo language if only Indo-European and East Asian languages are present). This method ensures the expanded dataset maximizes typological and genetic linguistic diversity with each new addition, improving the robustness and coverage of multilingual NLP models trained on it, rather than merely increasing the number of languages in a haphazard way.

ideas.title: Adapting Biodiversity Survey Methods for Multilingual Data Collection
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that high-quality instructional datasets must come from structurally homogeneous sources like WikiHow. Drawing an analogy from Ecology, where scientists survey biodiversity by combining structured transect sampling with unstructured environmental DNA (eDNA) collection from heterogeneous environments, we adapt this solution to NLP. The adapted research idea proposes to build procedural text datasets by designing systematic 'conceptual transects' to sample from the vast, unstructured multilingual web (e.g., blogs, forums) and employing 'linguistic eDNA' techniques to extract task-oriented phrases from noisy text. This methodology aims to capture genuine linguistic and cultural diversity in instructional content, moving beyond the structural homogeneity that limits current models' understanding and adaptability.

ideas.title: Ecological Resilience for Few-Shot Multilingual AI Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research direction applies ecological resilience principles to design decentralized, multilingual AI systems composed of many specialized, few-shot learning modules. Instead of a single large model, the ecosystem uses a 'species' of small, adaptable learners for different language pairs or tasks, each requiring minimal aligned data. This modularity allows the system to absorb shocks: if one module is attacked, fails, or encounters a low-resource language, others with overlapping or compensatory functions can maintain overall system performance. The synergy enables sustainable, scalable, and robust multilingual AI that is both data-efficient and inherently resistant to cascading failures.

ideas.title: Conceptually Grounded Cross-Lingual Alignment via Diverse Visual Proxies
ideas.description: This method moves beyond the constraint of identical image pairs by using a pre-trained multi-modal model (e.g., CLIP) to create a shared semantic-visual space. Text and images from different languages are encoded into this space, and alignment is learned by matching textual embeddings to the conceptual region defined by diverse, semantically related visual embeddings from the target language, not a single shared image. This leverages the grounding power of vision while accommodating cultural and contextual differences in imagery, resulting in more robust and semantically precise cross-lingual representations.

ideas.title: Performance boost solely from CoT content
ideas.description: Challenge that CoT's benefit comes only from its semantic content, not from structural or syntactic changes.

ideas.title: Sparse Language-Specialized Routing for Efficient Multilingual Inference
ideas.description: This approach designs an end-to-end multilingual transformer model that maintains translation quality while drastically reducing inference cost. It integrates a Mixture of Experts (MoE) layer where experts are sparsely activated sub-networks specialized for specific linguistic families or domains. A trainable, efficient routing network makes per-token decisions to use either a generic multilingual expert or a cheaper, specialized expert, dynamically minimizing FLOPs. The system is trained end-to-end with a load-balancing loss, ensuring it learns to make these routing decisions without degrading translation accuracy, directly optimizing the actual computational bottleneck.

ideas.title: Architectural Portfolio Theory for Robust AI Ensembles
ideas.description: This research seed proposes applying the principles of Financial Portfolio Theory to mitigate AI homogenization risks. The analogous problem in finance is over-reliance on a single asset class, solved by Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT), which constructs diversified portfolios to optimize the risk-return trade-off. We adapt this by conceptualizing different AI model architectures (e.g., transformers, state-space models, diffusion models, modular networks) as assets with distinct risk-return profiles. The research would involve developing metrics for architectural 'risk' (vulnerability to specific adversarial attacks, data distribution shifts, or bias amplification) and 'return' (task performance, efficiency). Using these metrics, we would create optimization frameworks to build ensembles of foundation models that are deliberately diverse in their architectural inductive biases, ensuring they fail in uncorrelated ways. This 'architectural portfolio' approach aims to produce AI systems that are fundamentally more robust and resilient than systems built on a single, homogenized architectural foundation, thereby directly addressing systemic risks from over-reliance on any one model paradigm.

ideas.title: A Continuous Adversarial Evaluation Framework for Uncovering Dynamic Failure Modes in Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research establishes a living evaluation ecosystem centered on continuous, adversarial probing of foundation models. Instead of a fixed benchmark suite, it creates a hybrid human-AI 'red team' platform that dynamically generates novel, edge-case, and distributionally shifted prompts and tasks, informed by real-world deployment logs and failure reports. The system prioritizes the discovery of unknown-unknowns and emergent failures, producing a constantly updated map of model vulnerabilities and robustness boundaries. This approach ensures evaluations remain relevant, challenging, and predictive of real-world risks beyond static leaderboard performance.

ideas.title: Control-Theoretic Steering for Stable, Non-Parametric Model Adaptation
ideas.description: This research proposes a control-theoretic framework for model adaptation that treats external steering inputs (e.g., system prompts, soft prompts, or lightweight external modules) as control signals applied to a frozen, black-box foundation model. The core innovation is using stability and robustness analysis from control theory (e.g., Lyapunov methods or robust control) to formally design and constrain these adaptation mechanisms. This ensures the adapted model's outputs remain bounded, predictable, and resistant to adversarial or distributional shifts, achieving reliable adaptation without fine-tuning. The result is a principled methodology for safe, real-time model personalization and task specialization that preserves model integrity and provides formal guarantees on behavior.

ideas.title: Cross-Lingual Alignment via Conceptual Graph Matching
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that image-based alignment is the gold standard for cross-lingual representation learning. Drawing an analogy from bioinformatics, where proteins are aligned by their functional roles in metabolic pathways rather than direct sequence similarity, we propose a method for language alignment that does not rely on shared images. The analogous solution involves mapping biological sequences to a shared functional ontology. Adapted to this context, we develop alignment methods that map textual units from different languages to a shared, abstract 'conceptual graph' constructed from multilingual knowledge bases (e.g., Wikidata triples, FrameNet frames, or distilled common sense relations). Alignment is achieved by optimizing the similarity of the relational contexts and structural positions of words within this graph, enabling the learning of cross-lingual embeddings based purely on conceptual and relational correspondence, without any visual grounding.

ideas.title: Behavioral Scaffolding: High-Fidelity Model Adaptation via Dynamic Input-Output Transformation
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel adaptation paradigm that maintains base model integrity while achieving fine-tuning-level behavioral alignment. A small, trainable 'scaffold' network learns to dynamically transform inputs and conditionally filter outputs of the frozen base model based on the target task. The scaffold is optimized using reinforcement learning against a reward model that encodes desired behavioral specifications, enabling deep task adaptation. This approach ensures high-fidelity adaptation, robust instruction-following, and internalization of new constraints, all without updating the base model's foundational weights.

ideas.description: This research investigates a hybrid architecture to overcome the brittleness of purely narrow-domain models. It pairs a small, curated-data specialist with a frozen, pre-trained generalist foundation model, using a learned router to decompose tasks. The system maintains deep domain expertise while preserving the cross-domain robustness and common-sense reasoning of broad training. This approach aims to produce models that are superior in a specialized domain without sacrificing the flexibility needed for real-world application.

ideas.title: A Hybrid Training Loop with a 'Symbolic Compiler' Stage
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of end-to-end differentiability by proposing a hybrid training loop inspired by classical compiler design. In compilers, a symbolic front-end (for parsing and semantics) is cleanly separated from an optimizing back-end. We adapt this by inserting a 'symbolic compiler' module into the training loop. This module applies non-differentiable symbolic reasoning or external knowledge base queries to model outputs, generating a refined intermediate representation that enforces logical consistency or factual accuracy. This symbolic IR then serves as the target for the subsequent differentiable loss calculation and gradient update, training the neural network to internalize the constraints imposed by the symbolic stage. The loop thus alternates between symbolic correction and differentiable optimization, integrating external knowledge without requiring the entire pipeline to be differentiable.

ideas.title: Algorithmic Efficiency as the New Scaling: Borrowing from Protein Folding
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that scale is the only path to AI capability by drawing an analogy from computational biology's protein folding problem. The analogous field is Protein Structure Prediction, where the long-standing solution of brute-force molecular dynamics simulation (analogous to scaling compute/data) was decisively overturned by DeepMind's AlphaFold2. Its solution was not more scale, but a novel algorithm that integrated evolutionary data and physical constraints into a deep learning architecture, achieving unprecedented accuracy with relatively efficient inference. Adapting this solution, the research proposes to treat core AI capabilities as a 'folding' problem solvable through algorithmic innovation rather than scaling. The adapted idea is to design novel, constrained model architectures with strong inductive biases—perhaps from symbolic reasoning, causal inference, or programmatic primitives—that force the efficient emergence of sophisticated capabilities in small-scale models. The objective is to achieve performance rivaling large-scale models but with radically reduced computational footprint, thereby challenging the prevailing scaling paradigm with a new priority on algorithmic insight and efficiency.

ideas.title: Econometrically-Guided Non-Differentiable Training for Socially-Optimal Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research direction proposes training foundation models using a hybrid objective that integrates standard gradient-based learning with non-differentiable modules for symbolic reasoning about economic externalities (e.g., fairness, market distortions, misinformation spread). The key innovation is using econometric models—trained on real-world deployment data—to quantify the causal economic impact of model outputs, converting this impact into a structured, symbolic constraint or reward. This feedback loop allows the model's training to be directly guided by econometric valuations, steering it towards generating outputs that are not just statistically plausible but also economically beneficial and socially optimal according to measurable criteria.

ideas.title: Co-Adaptive Training via Learned Differentiable Surrogates for Non-Differentiable Modules
ideas.description: This approach enables robust integration of non-differentiable symbolic reasoning by training a lightweight, parameterized surrogate model to mimic the symbolic module's input-output behavior in a differentiable way. The surrogate is meta-optimized concurrently with the main model to provide stable, useful gradients that guide learning while maintaining fidelity to the underlying symbolic logic. This creates a cohesive training loop where the neural and symbolic components co-adapt, preventing instability and ensuring the symbolic knowledge is effectively utilized. The system allows for backpropagation through the surrogate while periodically consulting and aligning with the ground-truth, non-differentiable module.

ideas.title: Establish a Standardized, Multi-Dimensional Evaluation Suite for Foundation Models Inspired by Clinical Trial Protocols
ideas.description: This research idea proposes to design comprehensive, systematic evaluation benchmarks for foundation models by drawing a direct analogy from the field of pharmaceutical development and clinical trials. The analogous problem—assessing the safety and efficacy of a new, complex intervention before public use—was solved through phased, standardized protocols (Phase I-III trials) that systematically test for desired effects, side effects, failure modes, and performance across diverse populations under controlled and real-world simulated conditions. Adapting this solution, the research will develop a formal, multi-phase evaluation suite for foundation models. This suite will define distinct evaluation phases with specific goals: initial capability and basic safety screening, rigorous stress-testing for robustness and bias on diverse datasets, and final real-world scenario simulations with adversarial testing. The outcome will be a standardized, repeatable protocol that produces a comprehensive profile of a model's capabilities, limitations, and failure modes, akin to a clinical trial report, providing a much more systematic and safety-oriented alternative to current benchmarking practices.

ideas.title: Learn Stable and Robust Latent Dynamics for Foundation Model Control
ideas.description: This approach addresses the core mismatch between classical control theory and foundation models by learning a stable, low-dimensional latent dynamics model from the model's internal activations. It formulates a joint optimization that trains the foundation model alongside a state observer and controller, using spectral constraints and contraction metrics to ensure the latent dynamics are provably stable. The system is adversarially stress-tested during training with perturbations in both input and latent space, guaranteeing robustness. This results in a model whose generative or reasoning trajectories are governed by a controllable and stable internal process, enabling formal guarantees on output behavior.

ideas.title: Autonomous Adversarial Robustness via Meta-Learning and Continuous Self-Improvement
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for foundation models that autonomously improves their own security and robustness through meta-learning and continuous adversarial simulation. The core system employs a meta-adversarial component tasked with inventing novel, unforeseen attack strategies by exploring the model's evolving failure modes and the expanding threat landscape. These discovered vulnerabilities are automatically converted into targeted training data and adversarial fine-tuning procedures, enabling the primary model to adapt and harden itself iteratively. This creates a proactive, self-strengthening defense cycle that moves beyond static robustness benchmarks towards inherent resilience against evolving threats.
