The core system uses neural architecture search and automated training algorithm discovery, constrained to a strict sub-1% FLOPs budget, to find novel model designs that maximize benchmark scores. Success is quantitatively measured by the Pareto frontier of performance vs. compute, directly testing whether algorithmic innovation can break the apparent scaling laws for a given level of capability.

ideas.title: Econometric Valuation of Efficiency: Measuring the Impact of Small-Scale and Algorithmic AI Innovations
ideas.description: This research program applies econometric models to measure the economic impact, productivity gains, and externalities (e.g., environmental, labor market) of AI systems that prioritize algorithmic efficiency or novel architectures over sheer scale. By constructing a comparative dataset of model capabilities, resource inputs, and deployment outcomes, it aims to isolate the marginal contribution of scale versus algorithmic innovation to real-world value. This allows for a direct, empirical challenge to the scale-centric paradigm by identifying conditions where small, efficient models provide superior or comparable net economic benefit. The resulting framework provides a tool for developers, regulators, and investors to optimize AI progress not just for capability, but for economic and social efficiency.

ideas.title: Immunological Adversarial Training for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This idea applies the immunological concept of vaccination to improve the security and robustness of foundation models against adversarial manipulation. In vaccinology, introducing a safe, modified version of a pathogen trains the immune system to build generalized, adaptive defenses. The adapted research idea proposes an 'Immunological Adversarial Training' framework. Here, models are not just hardened against a fixed set of known attacks but are 'vaccinated' during training with a curriculum of procedurally generated or strategically weakened adversarial perturbations. The goal is to stimulate the development of internal, generalizable defense mechanisms—analogous to antibodies—that enable the model to recognize the 'signature' of adversarial manipulation and reject it, thereby achieving a more robust and adaptive form of security against novel, unseen attacks.

ideas.title: Decentralized Control-Theoretic Frameworks for Stable Federated Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel control-theoretic framework for the decentralized or federated development and serving of foundation models. It treats the collective of participating agents (data holders, model trainers) as a distributed dynamical system, where model updates, parameter exchanges, and inference queries are control inputs and outputs. The core challenge is to design decentralized control algorithms—inspired by robust and adaptive control—that guarantee the stability, convergence, and output robustness of the globally emergent model despite heterogeneous data, non-IID distributions, and potentially adversarial participants. This moves beyond mere aggregation heuristics, providing formal assurances for safety and performance in a fully decentralized AI ecosystem.

ideas.title: Domain Adaptation via Geological Core Sample Correction
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from geophysics to solve the synthetic-to-real domain adaptation problem. In petrophysics, a 'core-calibrated' model uses high-fidelity physical rock core samples to correct distorted measurements from various downhole logging tools, bridging the gap between tool data and ground truth. Adapted to machine learning, a small set of real-world data acts as the 'core sample.' The research involves developing a physics-informed correction network that learns interpretable transformation functions to map features from abundant synthetic data onto the manifold defined by the limited real data, creating a more data-efficient and generalizable adaptation method than purely statistical domain alignment.

ideas.title: A Statistical Mechanics Framework for Predicting Emergent Capabilities via Phase Transitions
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to predicting emergent phenomena from statistical physics to the problem of emergent capabilities in AI. In physics, phase transitions (e.g., water boiling) are predictable using order parameters and scaling laws that describe how macroscopic properties change near a critical point, despite the system's microscopic complexity. The analogous solution is to create a theoretical framework for AI where a model's performance on a novel task is treated as an order parameter. The adapted idea is to develop scaling laws that predict how this 'capability order parameter' changes as a function of scale factors (model parameters, dataset size, compute). The goal is to identify critical scaling exponents and thresholds from experiments on smaller models, enabling the prediction of which capabilities will emerge and at what scale they will sharply improve, providing a principled theory for the 'phase transitions' observed in foundation models.

ideas.title: A Multi-Method Causal Synthesis Framework for Bounding Foundation Model Impact
ideas.description: This research develops a robust framework to bound the economic value and externalities of foundation models without relying on an unattainable perfect counterfactual. It integrates diverse causal evidence from natural experiments, granular adoption data, and expert judgment within a Bayesian synthesis model. The output is not a single point estimate but a probability distribution of impacts, explicitly quantifying uncertainty from identification challenges. This provides policymakers and researchers with a more honest and actionable assessment of model effects.

ideas.title: Adaptation via Epigenetic Control: A Non-Fine-Tuning Framework for Model Specialization
ideas.description: Challenging the assumption that model adaptation is primarily achieved via fine-tuning, this idea draws an analogy from molecular biology's epigenetic mechanisms. In genetics, organisms use chemical tags to regulate gene expression without changing their core DNA sequence, enabling adaptable, reversible specialization. The analogous solution is adapted by proposing a framework where a large base model's outputs are specialized not by updating its weights, but by applying a learned, task-specific 'epigenetic' control layer. This layer could take the form of a sparse mask on model components or a lightweight module that modulates activations, allowing for flexible, integrity-preserving adaptation to new challenges while maintaining the original model's capabilities and preventing catastrophic forgetting.

ideas.title: Control-Theoretic Orchestration of Diverse Model Ensembles for Robust AI Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel control-theoretic framework to actively manage and orchestrate a diverse set of foundation model architectures, treating the ensemble as a dynamic, multi-agent system. By modeling interactions and performance feedback loops, the framework uses adaptive control strategies to dynamically allocate tasks, blend outputs, and guide fine-tuning, ensuring the overall system's stability and robustness against distribution shifts or adversarial inputs. This moves beyond static architectural diversity by introducing formal guarantees on output consistency and resilience, mitigating homogenization risks not just through variety but through principled, feedback-driven coordination. The result is a more reliable and adaptable AI ecosystem where diversity is systematically leveraged to maintain stable, high-performance operation under uncertainty.

ideas.title: Protocol-Based Adaptive Governance Infrastructure for Dynamic AI System Oversight
ideas.description: This research designs and prototypes a technical and contractual infrastructure for adaptive AI governance, moving beyond static legal frameworks. It develops a suite of measurable performance indicators for societal impact and misuse, linked to automated governance protocols that can be activated by model developers, deployers, or regulators. The system enables dynamic, proportional responses to observed risks—such as escalating bias or security vulnerabilities—through technical interventions (e.g., API throttling, mandatory model cards) and contractual obligations embedded in model access agreements. This creates a responsive, enforceable oversight mechanism that evolves with the technology it governs.

ideas.title: Incentivized Model Diversity via Dynamic Ensemble Markets
ideas.description: This research designs a system to counteract the economic drivers of AI homogenization by making architectural and functional diversity economically sustainable. It develops technical methods for real-time, cost-aware routing and composition of queries across a heterogeneous pool of smaller, specialized foundation models. Concurrently, it designs and tests market mechanisms that reward providers for contributing unique capabilities, ensuring the ecosystem supports multiple architectures. The outcome is a framework where diversity is not just technically possible but is actively incentivized, fundamentally reducing systemic risk from over-reliance on a single model.

ideas.title: Adapting the Precautionary Principle and Adaptive Management from Environmental Governance for AI Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research seed proposes designing new legal and ethical governance frameworks for foundation models by drawing an analogy from environmental governance. The analogous field is Environmental Science, specifically the governance of ecological systems facing novel, high-stakes, and poorly understood risks. The analogous solution combines the Precautionary Principle—which mandates proactive restraint in the face of uncertain but potentially severe harm—with Adaptive Management, a structured process of implementing policies as experiments, monitoring outcomes, and iteratively updating rules based on evidence. This approach is institutionalized in bodies like those under the Cartagena Protocol on Biosafety. Adapted to foundation models, the research would develop a framework where high-capacity models undergo a legally defined governance cycle. This includes a precautionary pre-deployment containment phase with rigorous, interdisciplinary impact audits, followed by a monitored release phase where real-world societal effects (equity, misuse, economic disruption) are tracked by an independent body. The governance framework would be designed to legally mandate iterative model adjustments, usage restrictions, or recalls based on this monitored evidence, creating a dynamic and learning-based system for managing the societal impact of AI.

ideas.title: Apply Metabolic Control Analysis to Foundation Model Training Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Systems Biology, specifically Metabolic Control Analysis (MCA), to the problem of ensuring stability and robustness in foundation model training. MCA provides a framework to quantify distributed control and sensitivity in complex networks, moving beyond the search for a single point of failure. By treating the neural network's architecture and training process as an analogous biochemical network, we can develop formal metrics to measure how perturbations (e.g., weight changes, data batch variations, learning rate adjustments) propagate and which components exert the most control over output stability and training loss. This allows for a principled, control-theoretic analysis to identify critical leverage points for stabilizing training, improving robustness to hyperparameter choices, and preventing training divergence or output degradation.

ideas.title: Adapting Immune System Memory Mechanisms for AI Ecosystem Resilience
ideas.description: This research adapts the immunological memory solution from immunology to create resilient AI ecosystems. In immunology, the adaptive immune system creates memory cells after an initial pathogen exposure, enabling a faster and stronger response upon re-infection. We propose to implement an analogous 'AI immune memory'—a subsystem that records the characteristics of previous model failures, adversarial attacks, or performance degradation events. When monitoring detects a threat pattern matching a stored signature, the ecosystem can trigger pre-programmed, validated responses such as isolating compromised models, activating hardened backup models, or applying specific corrective data filters. This moves resilience from purely reactive to proactively prepared, reducing system downtime and vulnerability to repeat or similar attacks, much like immunological memory provides long-term protection to biological organisms.

ideas.title: Byzantine-Robust Federated Development with Verifiable Contribution Consensus
ideas.description: This system designs a decentralized foundation model framework with inherent resistance to coordinated poisoning and sybil attacks. It establishes a permissioned, reputation-weighted federation where participants must cryptographically commit to their data and gradient contributions, which are then verified by randomly selected, rotating committees of other nodes. The core innovation is a consensus mechanism that not only aggregates updates but also validates their statistical alignment with a resilient, evolving model of expected non-adversarial behavior, automatically isolating and slashing the stake of colluding actors. This ensures the integrity of the decentralized training process while preserving its core benefits of distributed control and data sovereignty.

ideas.title: Adapting Environmental Impact Valuation to Measure AI Externalities
ideas.description: This research adapts solution methods from environmental economics—specifically developed to value non-market environmental goods and externalities—to the problem of quantifying the economic value and externalities of foundation models. In environmental economics, techniques like contingent valuation, hedonic pricing, and social cost-benefit analysis are used to assign monetary value to intangible impacts such as clean air or the social cost of pollution. The adapted idea applies these econometric methods to AI: using contingent valuation surveys to estimate the economic value placed on AI safety, fairness, or transparency by stakeholders; and employing hedonic pricing models to analyze how the deployment of specific foundation models affects related economic indicators (e.g., productivity premiums, wage shifts, or software pricing), thereby isolating and quantifying the model's direct economic impact and its broader positive/negative externalities.

ideas.title: Governance-Driven Adversarial Robustness: Formalizing and Enforcing Security as a Sociotechnical Property
ideas.description: This research direction develops methods to translate high-level legal and ethical governance principles—such as equity, accountability, and non-maleficence—into concrete, testable technical specifications for adversarial robustness. It moves beyond creating defensive algorithms to designing integrated sociotechnical systems where governance frameworks formally define security requirements (e.g., 'resilience against manipulation in high-stakes domains'), and technical teams implement verifiable mechanisms to meet them. This enables external auditing and certification of model security against a societal risk profile, creating accountability loops where robustness failures can be traced to breaches of governed standards. The synergy transforms security from a model-centric, lab-based challenge into a governed, lifecycle-wide property that aligns technical defenses with mandated societal safeguards.

It defines 'scale' as a multi-dimensional experimental variable and designs large-scale, controlled ablation studies across model families, data distributions, and architectures to isolate the drivers of qualitative capability jumps. The outcome is a set of formal, empirically-validated scaling laws that specify the necessary conditions for specific emergent skills, enabling pre-training predictions about capability thresholds.

ideas.description: This approach moves beyond adapting from a single synthetic source to a monolithic real target. It treats the real-world deployment environment as a dynamic mixture of latent sub-domains (e.g., clear/rainy, day/night). The system performs continuous, test-time adaptation by clustering incoming real data streams to identify these sub-domains on-the-fly. It then applies tailored feature alignment and uses a consistency regularization loss that is invariant to shifts within the target mixture, ensuring robust performance across the entire, non-stationary real-world distribution.

ideas.title: A Federated, Peer-to-Peer Model Training Protocol Inspired by Ecological Nutrient Cycling
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the centralization assumption in AI by proposing a fully decentralized framework for developing foundation models, inspired by nutrient cycling in ecosystems. In ecology, decentralized, peer-to-peer exchanges of essential elements create a resilient, self-regulating system. We adapt this solution by designing a federated, peer-to-peer training protocol where model parameters or updates are treated as 'nutrients.' Nodes compute updates on local data and engage in direct, structured exchanges with peers, using cryptographic and game-theoretic mechanisms for validation and incentive alignment. This creates a distributed, collaborative learning process that converges on a robust foundation model without a central authority ever aggregating data or fully controlling the training pipeline, thereby inverting the current paradigm of centralized development and control.

ideas.title: Apply Biochemical Pathway Verification to Neural Network Activation Patterns
ideas.description: This idea adapts formal verification methods from systems biology and metabolic engineering to foundation model safety. In metabolic engineering, formal constraint-based methods like flux balance analysis are used to mathematically prove that engineered biochemical pathways cannot produce toxic byproducts, analyzing the network's stoichiometry to guarantee safe operation. Similarly, we can treat a neural network's internal activation patterns as a computational 'reaction network' and develop formal methods to prove that certain harmful output states (e.g., toxic, biased, or factually incorrect generations) cannot be reached. This involves creating mathematical constraints on the model's forward pass operations to provably guarantee that activation pathways leading to unsafe outputs are impossible, providing a rigorous safety verification framework analogous to biochemical pathway safety certification.
